details.etymology: Derived from Middle High German 'verantworten' (to answer for), combining 'ver-' (prefix) and 'Antwort' (answer).
examples: Er trägt die Verantwortung für das Projekt. (He bears the responsibility for the project.)
Verantwortung zu übernehmen ist ein Zeichen von Reife. (Taking responsibility is a sign of maturity.)
details.usage: Commonly used in compounds like 'Eigenverantwortung' (personal responsibility) and 'Verantwortungsbewusstsein' (sense of responsibility).

details.etymology: Derived from the verb 'behandeln' (to treat/handle), which comes from 'Hand' (hand), reflecting the idea of handling something.
examples: Die Behandlung im Krankenhaus war sehr gut. (The treatment at the hospital was very good.)
Er braucht eine ärztliche Behandlung. (He needs medical treatment.)
details.usage: Widely used in medical contexts but also means 'treatment' of a topic; common compound: 'Sonderbehandlung' (special treatment).

details.etymology: Compound of 'ein' (in) and 'Druck' (pressure/impression), calqued from Latin 'impressio'.
examples: Der erste Eindruck ist oft entscheidend. (The first impression is often decisive.)
Die Stadt hat einen starken Eindruck auf mich gemacht. (The city made a strong impression on me.)
details.usage: Very common in the phrase 'einen guten Eindruck machen' (to make a good impression); also used in 'eindrucksvoll' (impressive).

details.etymology: From Middle High German 'geselleschaft', derived from 'Geselle' (companion), originally meaning a group of companions.
examples: Wir leben in einer modernen Gesellschaft. (We live in a modern society.)
Er gründete eine Gesellschaft mit beschränkter Haftung. (He founded a limited liability company.)
details.usage: Used both for 'society' and 'company' (business); abbreviation 'GmbH' stands for 'Gesellschaft mit beschränkter Haftung'.

details.etymology: Compound of 'Grund' (ground/basis) and 'Lage' (position/layer), literally meaning 'base layer'.
examples: Diese Studie bildet die Grundlage für weitere Forschung. (This study forms the basis for further research.)
Ohne gute Grundlagen kann man nichts aufbauen. (Without good foundations, you can't build anything.)
details.usage: Frequently used in academic and professional contexts; 'Grundlagenkurs' means a foundational course.

details.etymology: Compound of 'zusammen' (together) and 'Hang' (from 'hängen', to hang), meaning things that hang together or are connected.
examples: In diesem Zusammenhang möchte ich etwas erwähnen. (In this context, I would like to mention something.)
Es gibt einen klaren Zusammenhang zwischen Bildung und Einkommen. (There is a clear connection between education and income.)
details.usage: Very common in formal writing; 'in diesem Zusammenhang' (in this context) is a standard phrase in essays and speeches.

details.etymology: Derived from 'ausbilden' (to train/educate), combining 'aus' (out) and 'bilden' (to form/shape).
examples: Sie macht eine Ausbildung zur Krankenschwester. (She is training to become a nurse.)
Eine gute Ausbildung ist wichtig für die Zukunft. (A good education is important for the future.)
details.usage: In Germany, 'Ausbildung' specifically refers to vocational training (dual system), distinct from university education ('Studium').

details.etymology: From 'enttäuschen' (to disappoint), literally meaning 'to un-deceive', from 'ent-' (un-) and 'täuschen' (to deceive).
examples: Die Enttäuschung war groß, als das Spiel abgesagt wurde. (The disappointment was great when the game was canceled.)
Er konnte seine Enttäuschung nicht verbergen. (He could not hide his disappointment.)
details.usage: Interestingly, the etymological meaning suggests removing an illusion, though it is used purely as 'disappointment' in modern German.

details.etymology: From Middle High German 'verglich', related to 'gleich' (equal/same), meaning to make things equal or compare them.
examples: Im Vergleich zu letztem Jahr sind die Preise gestiegen. (Compared to last year, prices have risen.)
Der Vergleich hinkt ein wenig. (The comparison is a bit flawed.)
details.usage: Used both for 'comparison' and as a legal term for 'settlement'; 'im Vergleich zu' (compared to) is an extremely common phrase.

details.etymology: From Middle High German 'bedingunge', related to 'bedingen' (to stipulate), originally connected to 'Ding' (thing/matter).
examples: Unter diesen Bedingungen kann ich nicht arbeiten. (I cannot work under these conditions.)
Die Bedingung für den Vertrag ist klar. (The condition of the contract is clear.)
details.usage: Common in both everyday and legal language; 'Arbeitsbedingungen' (working conditions) and 'Wetterbedingungen' (weather conditions) are frequent compounds.

details.etymology: From the verb 'wirken' (to have an effect), derived from Old High German 'wirken' (to work/act).
examples: Das Medikament zeigt bereits seine Wirkung. (The medication is already showing its effect.)
Die Wirkung seiner Rede war enorm. (The impact of his speech was enormous.)
details.usage: Central to many compounds: 'Nebenwirkung' (side effect), 'Auswirkung' (consequence), 'Wechselwirkung' (interaction).

details.etymology: From 'entscheiden' (to decide), derived from 'scheiden' (to separate/part), implying separating options to reach a conclusion.
examples: Das war eine schwierige Entscheidung. (That was a difficult decision.)
Die Entscheidung liegt bei dir. (The decision is up to you.)
details.usage: One of the most frequently used abstract nouns in German; 'Entscheidungsträger' (decision-maker) is a common compound.

details.etymology: From 'entwickeln' (to develop), literally meaning 'to unwrap', from 'ent-' (un-) and 'wickeln' (to wrap).
examples: Die Entwicklung der Technologie schreitet schnell voran. (The development of technology is progressing rapidly.)
Das Kind zeigt eine positive Entwicklung. (The child is showing positive development.)
details.usage: Extremely common in professional and academic German; 'Entwicklungsland' (developing country) and 'Softwareentwicklung' (software development) are standard compounds.

details.etymology: Compound of 'ab' (off/away) and 'Sicht' (sight/view), originally meaning a view directed toward something specific.
examples: Er hat die Absicht, nach Berlin zu ziehen. (He has the intention of moving to Berlin.)
Das war keine Absicht! (That was not intentional!)
details.usage: Often used in the phrase 'mit Absicht' (on purpose/intentionally) and 'absichtlich' (deliberate) is the derived adjective.

details.etymology: From Middle High German 'anlaz', related to 'anlassen' (to start/set going), originally meaning an impulse to begin.
examples: Was ist der Anlass für die Feier? (What is the occasion for the celebration?)
Es gibt keinen Anlass zur Sorge. (There is no reason for concern.)
details.usage: Used both for 'occasion' (festive) and 'reason/cause'; 'aus gegebenem Anlass' (for a given reason) is a common formal phrase.

details.etymology: Compound of 'an' (at/to) and 'Spruch' (saying/verdict), originally a legal claim made by speaking.
examples: Sie hat hohe Ansprüche an sich selbst. (She has high expectations of herself.)
Er hat Anspruch auf Urlaub. (He is entitled to vacation.)
details.usage: Used in legal contexts for 'claim/entitlement' and in everyday speech for 'expectation'; 'anspruchsvoll' (demanding/sophisticated) is a common adjective.

details.etymology: From 'ausnehmen' (to take out/exempt), combining 'aus' (out) and 'nehmen' (to take).
examples: Jede Regel hat ihre Ausnahme. (Every rule has its exception.)
Mit Ausnahme von Maria waren alle da. (With the exception of Maria, everyone was there.)
details.usage: The saying 'Ausnahmen bestätigen die Regel' (exceptions prove the rule) is very well known in German.

details.etymology: Compound of 'aus' (out) and 'Sage' (from 'sagen', to say), meaning something spoken out or stated.
examples: Die Aussage des Zeugen war entscheidend. (The witness's statement was decisive.)
Was ist die Aussage dieses Textes? (What is the message of this text?)
details.usage: Used both in legal contexts (testimony/statement) and in literary analysis (message); 'eine Aussage machen' means to make a statement.

details.etymology: From 'bewegen' (to move), derived from Old High German 'biwegen', related to 'Weg' (way/path).
examples: Regelmäßige Bewegung ist gut für die Gesundheit. (Regular exercise is good for health.)
Die politische Bewegung gewann viele Anhänger. (The political movement gained many followers.)
details.usage: Means both physical movement/exercise and a social or political movement; 'Bewegungsfreiheit' (freedom of movement) is a common compound.

details.etymology: From 'beziehen' (to relate to), derived from 'ziehen' (to pull/draw), suggesting a connection drawn between things.
examples: Sie führen eine glückliche Beziehung. (They are in a happy relationship.)
In dieser Beziehung hast du recht. (In this regard, you are right.)
details.usage: Used for romantic relationships and also for connections in general; 'Beziehungsstatus' (relationship status) is a modern compound.

details.etymology: From Old High German 'druc', related to 'drücken' (to press), cognate with English 'thrust'.
examples: Er steht unter großem Druck bei der Arbeit. (He is under great pressure at work.)
Der Druck dieses Buches ist sehr hochwertig. (The print of this book is very high quality.)
details.usage: Has two distinct meanings: 'pressure' (Drücke) and 'print' (Drucke); compounds include 'Blutdruck' (blood pressure) and 'Buchdruck' (book printing).

details.etymology: Compound of 'eigen' (own) and '-schaft' (suffix denoting a state/quality), meaning a quality that belongs to something.
examples: Ehrlichkeit ist eine gute Eigenschaft. (Honesty is a good quality.)
Dieses Material hat besondere Eigenschaften. (This material has special properties.)
details.usage: Used for personal character traits and also for physical or chemical properties of materials.

details.etymology: Calque from Latin 'influentia', combining 'ein' (in) and 'Fluss' (flow), meaning something flowing in.
examples: Sie hat großen Einfluss auf ihre Kollegen. (She has great influence on her colleagues.)
Der kulturelle Einfluss Frankreichs ist deutlich sichtbar. (The cultural influence of France is clearly visible.)
details.usage: Very common in discussions of power and culture; 'einflussreich' (influential) is the derived adjective.

details.etymology: From 'einnehmen' (to take in/capture), combining 'ein' (in) and 'nehmen' (to take).
examples: Die Einnahmen des Unternehmens sind gestiegen. (The company's revenue has increased.)
Die Einnahme von Medikamenten sollte regelmäßig erfolgen. (The intake of medication should be done regularly.)
details.usage: Means both 'revenue/income' (usually plural) and 'intake' (of medication); opposite of 'Ausgabe' (expenditure).

details.etymology: From 'einsetzen' (to deploy/use), combining 'ein' (in) and 'setzen' (to set/place).
examples: Die Feuerwehr war im Einsatz. (The fire department was on duty.)
Sein Einsatz für die Umwelt ist bewundernswert. (His commitment to the environment is admirable.)
details.usage: Versatile word meaning deployment, commitment, effort, or stake (in gambling); 'Polizeieinsatz' (police operation) is a common compound.

details.etymology: From 'eintreten' (to enter/step in), combining 'ein' (in) and 'Tritt' (step).
examples: Der Eintritt kostet zehn Euro. (The admission costs ten euros.)
Bei Eintritt der Dunkelheit gingen wir nach Hause. (At the onset of darkness, we went home.)
details.usage: Means both 'admission/entry' (to a place) and 'onset/occurrence' (of an event); 'Eintrittskarte' (admission ticket) is a standard compound.

details.etymology: From 'empfehlen' (to recommend), derived from Old High German 'inpfelahan' (to entrust).
examples: Haben Sie eine Empfehlung für ein gutes Restaurant? (Do you have a recommendation for a good restaurant?)
Auf Empfehlung meines Arztes treibe ich mehr Sport. (On my doctor's recommendation, I exercise more.)
details.usage: Common in both formal and everyday contexts; 'Empfehlungsschreiben' (letter of recommendation) is frequently used in professional settings.

details.etymology: From 'erkennen' (to recognize/realize), derived from Old High German 'irkennida', related to 'kennen' (to know).
examples: Diese Erkenntnis hat mein Leben verändert. (This realization changed my life.)
Die wissenschaftlichen Erkenntnisse sind eindeutig. (The scientific findings are clear.)
details.usage: A key concept in philosophy (Kant's 'Erkenntnistheorie' = epistemology); used in everyday language for 'insight' or 'finding'.

details.etymology: From 'ernähren' (to nourish/feed), derived from 'nähren' (to nourish), related to Old High German 'nerien'.
examples: Eine gesunde Ernährung ist sehr wichtig. (A healthy diet is very important.)
Er hat seine Ernährung komplett umgestellt. (He completely changed his diet.)
details.usage: A very common topic word in German media; 'Ernährungsberater' (nutritionist) and 'Ernährungsweise' (dietary habit) are standard compounds.

details.etymology: From 'fähig' (capable), derived from a Middle High German root meaning 'apt/suitable', with the suffix '-keit' forming an abstract noun.
examples: Er hat die Fähigkeit, komplexe Probleme zu lösen. (He has the ability to solve complex problems.)
Ihre sozialen Fähigkeiten sind beeindruckend. (Her social skills are impressive.)
details.usage: Often used in job descriptions and educational contexts; similar to 'Kompetenz' but more focused on innate or developed ability.

details.etymology: From 'fordern' (to demand), derived from Old High German 'fordaron', related to 'fördern' (to promote).
examples: Die Gewerkschaft stellte neue Forderungen. (The union made new demands.)
Die Forderung nach Gleichberechtigung wird lauter. (The demand for equal rights is growing louder.)
details.usage: Used in political, legal, and financial contexts; not to be confused with 'Förderung' (promotion/support), despite similar spelling.

details.etymology: From 'forschen' (to research), derived from Old High German 'forskon' (to investigate/ask).
examples: Die Forschung hat große Fortschritte gemacht. (Research has made great progress.)
Er arbeitet in der medizinischen Forschung. (He works in medical research.)
details.usage: Central to academic German; 'Forschung und Entwicklung' (research and development, R&D) is a standard phrase in industry.

details.etymology: From 'frei' (free), derived from Old High German 'friheit', with the suffix '-heit' forming an abstract noun, cognate with English 'freedom'.
examples: Die Freiheit der Presse ist ein Grundrecht. (Freedom of the press is a fundamental right.)
Er genießt seine Freiheit nach dem Studium. (He enjoys his freedom after university.)
details.usage: A politically and culturally significant word in Germany, especially after reunification; 'Redefreiheit' (freedom of speech) and 'Religionsfreiheit' (freedom of religion) are key compounds.

details.etymology: Borrowed from French 'garantie', which itself came from Frankish 'warand' (guarantee/protection).
examples: Das Gerät hat zwei Jahre Garantie. (The device has a two-year warranty.)
Es gibt keine Garantie für Erfolg. (There is no guarantee of success.)
details.usage: Used for both product warranties and figurative guarantees; legally distinct from 'Gewährleistung' (statutory warranty) in German consumer law.

details.etymology: From Old High German 'gidanc', related to 'denken' (to think), with the prefix 'ge-' indicating a result of thinking.
examples: Der Gedanke an den Urlaub macht mich glücklich. (The thought of vacation makes me happy.)
Was sind deine Gedanken dazu? (What are your thoughts on that?)
details.usage: A weak masculine noun (der Gedanke, den Gedanken, dem Gedanken); 'Gedankenfreiheit' (freedom of thought) and 'gedankenlos' (thoughtless) are common derivatives.

details.etymology: From Old High German 'gisezzida', related to 'setzen' (to set/place), literally meaning something that has been set down or established.
examples: Dieses Gesetz tritt nächsten Monat in Kraft. (This law takes effect next month.)
Niemand steht über dem Gesetz. (Nobody is above the law.)
details.usage: Fundamental term in legal German; 'Grundgesetz' (Basic Law) is Germany's constitution, and 'gesetzlich' (legal/statutory) is the derived adjective.

details.etymology: From Old High German 'giwalt', related to 'walten' (to rule/govern), cognate with English 'wield'.
examples: Gewalt ist keine Lösung. (Violence is not a solution.)
Die drei Gewalten des Staates sind Exekutive, Legislative und Judikative. (The three powers of the state are executive, legislative, and judicial.)
details.usage: Means both 'violence' and 'power/authority'; 'Gewaltenteilung' (separation of powers) is a key political concept.

details.etymology: From Middle High German 'gewizzen', related to 'wissen' (to know), originally meaning inner knowledge of right and wrong.
examples: Er hat ein schlechtes Gewissen. (He has a guilty conscience.)
Mein Gewissen erlaubt mir das nicht. (My conscience does not allow me to do that.)
details.usage: Common in phrases like 'gutes/schlechtes Gewissen' (good/bad conscience) and 'gewissenhaft' (conscientious).

details.etymology: From 'gleich' (equal/same), derived from Old High German 'gilih', with the suffix '-heit' forming an abstract noun.
examples: Die Gleichheit aller Menschen ist ein wichtiges Prinzip. (The equality of all people is an important principle.)
Gleichheit vor dem Gesetz ist ein Grundrecht. (Equality before the law is a fundamental right.)
details.usage: A central concept in German politics and law; related words include 'Gleichberechtigung' (equal rights) and 'Chancengleichheit' (equal opportunity).

details.etymology: Compound of 'Haus' (house) and '-halt' (from 'halten', to hold/keep), originally meaning keeping a household.
examples: Sie führt den Haushalt allein. (She runs the household alone.)
Der Bundeshaushalt wurde verabschiedet. (The federal budget was passed.)
details.usage: Means both 'household' and 'budget' (government); 'Haushaltsplan' (budget plan) and 'Haushaltsgeld' (housekeeping money) are common compounds.

details.etymology: From 'herkommen' (to come from), combining 'her' (from/hither) and 'Kunft' (related to 'kommen', to come).
examples: Woher kennen Sie Ihre Herkunft? (How do you know your origin?)
Unabhängig von der Herkunft sind alle willkommen. (Regardless of origin, everyone is welcome.)
details.usage: Used for both geographical origin and ancestry; 'Herkunftsland' (country of origin) is a common compound in immigration discussions.

details.etymology: From 'hoffen' (to hope), derived from Middle Low German 'hopen', with the suffix '-ung' forming a noun.
examples: Die Hoffnung stirbt zuletzt. (Hope dies last.)
Er gab die Hoffnung nie auf. (He never gave up hope.)
details.usage: Very common in everyday and literary language; 'hoffnungsvoll' (hopeful) and 'hoffnungslos' (hopeless) are standard derivatives.

details.etymology: Borrowed from French 'industrie', ultimately from Latin 'industria' (diligence/activity).
examples: Die Industrie hat viele Arbeitsplätze geschaffen. (Industry has created many jobs.)
Die Automobilindustrie ist sehr wichtig für Deutschland. (The automobile industry is very important for Germany.)
details.usage: Germany is known as an industrial powerhouse; 'Industriegebiet' (industrial area) and 'Industrieland' (industrialized country) are frequent compounds.

details.etymology: Borrowed from Latin 'clima', which came from Greek 'klima' (inclination/region), originally referring to the tilt of the earth.
examples: Das Klima in Deutschland ist gemäßigt. (The climate in Germany is temperate.)
Der Klimawandel ist eine große Herausforderung. (Climate change is a major challenge.)
details.usage: Also used figuratively for atmosphere, as in 'Arbeitsklima' (work atmosphere); 'Klimaschutz' (climate protection) is a major topic in German politics.

details.etymology: Borrowed from Latin 'conflictus' (collision/clash), from 'confligere' (to strike together).
examples: Der Konflikt zwischen den beiden Ländern eskalierte. (The conflict between the two countries escalated.)
Es gibt einen Konflikt zwischen Arbeit und Familie. (There is a conflict between work and family.)
details.usage: Used for both armed conflicts and interpersonal disagreements; 'Konfliktlösung' (conflict resolution) is a standard compound.

details.etymology: Borrowed from Latin 'critica', from Greek 'kritike' (the art of judging), related to 'krinein' (to judge/separate).
examples: Die Kritik an der Regierung wächst. (Criticism of the government is growing.)
Der Film bekam gute Kritiken. (The film received good reviews.)
details.usage: Means both 'criticism' and 'review' (of art, film, etc.); 'Kritiker' (critic) and 'kritisch' (critical) are common derivatives.

details.etymology: Borrowed from Latin 'cultura' (cultivation/tending), from 'colere' (to cultivate).
examples: Die deutsche Kultur ist vielfältig. (German culture is diverse.)
Es ist wichtig, andere Kulturen zu respektieren. (It is important to respect other cultures.)
details.usage: A broad term encompassing arts, traditions, and civilization; 'Kulturerbe' (cultural heritage) and 'multikulturell' (multicultural) are widely used.

details.etymology: From 'Leiden' (suffering) and '-schaft' (suffix), originally meaning a state of suffering or intense emotion, later shifting to 'passion'.
examples: Kochen ist seine größte Leidenschaft. (Cooking is his greatest passion.)
Sie spielt Klavier mit großer Leidenschaft. (She plays piano with great passion.)
details.usage: The etymological connection to 'suffering' reflects the Romantic-era view that passion involves emotional turmoil; 'leidenschaftlich' (passionate) is the adjective form.

details.etymology: Compound of 'Maß' (measure) and 'Nahme' (from 'nehmen', to take), literally meaning 'taking a measure'.
examples: Die Regierung ergreift neue Maßnahmen gegen die Krise. (The government is taking new measures against the crisis.)
Welche Maßnahmen schlagen Sie vor? (What measures do you suggest?)
details.usage: Very frequent in political and administrative language; 'Schutzmaßnahme' (protective measure) and 'Sparmaßnahme' (austerity measure) are common compounds.

details.etymology: Compound of 'miss-' (prefix meaning wrong/bad) and 'Erfolg' (success), literally 'bad success' or failure.
examples: Nach vielen Misserfolgen gab er nicht auf. (After many failures, he did not give up.)
Der Film war ein kommerzieller Misserfolg. (The film was a commercial failure.)
details.usage: Direct antonym of 'Erfolg' (success); German commonly forms negative words with the prefix 'miss-' or 'Miss-'.

details.etymology: From 'mitteilen' (to inform/communicate), combining 'mit' (with) and 'teilen' (to share/divide).
examples: Ich habe eine wichtige Mitteilung für Sie. (I have an important message for you.)
Die Mitteilung wurde per E-Mail verschickt. (The notification was sent by email.)
details.usage: Used in formal contexts for announcements and notices; 'Pressemitteilung' (press release) is a very common compound.

details.etymology: From Old High German 'mittil' (middle/means), related to 'Mitte' (middle), reflecting the idea of an intermediary or instrument.
examples: Uns fehlen die finanziellen Mittel. (We lack the financial means.)
Dieses Mittel hilft gegen Kopfschmerzen. (This remedy helps against headaches.)
details.usage: Extremely versatile: means 'means', 'remedy', 'resources', or 'agent'; compounds include 'Lebensmittel' (food), 'Heilmittel' (remedy), 'Schmerzmittel' (painkiller).

details.etymology: From 'nachweisen' (to prove/demonstrate), combining 'nach' (after/following) and 'weisen' (to show/point).
examples: Bitte bringen Sie einen Nachweis über Ihr Einkommen. (Please bring proof of your income.)
Der Nachweis wurde erbracht. (The proof was provided.)
details.usage: Common in bureaucratic and scientific contexts; 'Impfnachweis' (vaccination proof) became widely known during the pandemic.

details.etymology: Compound of 'neu' (new) and 'Gier' (greed/craving), literally 'craving for the new'.
examples: Seine Neugier trieb ihn an, mehr zu erfahren. (His curiosity drove him to learn more.)
Aus reiner Neugier habe ich nachgefragt. (Out of pure curiosity, I asked.)
details.usage: Also spelled 'Neugierde' with identical meaning; 'neugierig' (curious) is the very common adjective form.

details.etymology: From Old High German 'nuz' (use/benefit), related to the verb 'nutzen' (to use), cognate with English 'nut' in obsolete sense of 'use'.
examples: Was ist der Nutzen dieser Maßnahme? (What is the benefit of this measure?)
Der Nutzen überwiegt die Kosten. (The benefit outweighs the costs.)
details.usage: Often paired with 'Kosten' in 'Kosten-Nutzen-Analyse' (cost-benefit analysis); 'nützlich' (useful) is the common adjective.

details.etymology: Borrowed from Latin 'politica', from Greek 'politike' (the art of governing), related to 'polis' (city-state).
examples: Er interessiert sich sehr für Politik. (He is very interested in politics.)
Die Politik muss handeln. (Politicians/The government must act.)
details.usage: Can refer to politics in general or to specific policies; 'Politiker' (politician) and 'politisch' (political) are essential derivatives.

details.etymology: Borrowed from Latin 'principium' (beginning/foundation), from 'princeps' (first/chief).
examples: Im Prinzip hast du recht. (In principle, you are right.)
Er handelt nach festen Prinzipien. (He acts according to firm principles.)
details.usage: The phrase 'im Prinzip' (in principle) is extremely common in spoken German; 'prinzipiell' (in principle/fundamentally) is the adjectival form.

details.etymology: Borrowed from Latin 'processus' (progress/advance), from 'procedere' (to go forward).
examples: Der Prozess gegen den Angeklagten beginnt morgen. (The trial against the defendant begins tomorrow.)
Der Heilungsprozess dauert mehrere Wochen. (The healing process takes several weeks.)
details.usage: Means both 'trial/lawsuit' (legal) and 'process' (general); 'Gerichtsprozess' (court trial) and 'Arbeitsprozess' (work process) are common compounds.

details.etymology: Borrowed from Latin 'reactio', combining 're-' (back) and 'actio' (action), meaning an action in response.
examples: Seine Reaktion hat mich überrascht. (His reaction surprised me.)
Die Reaktionen auf das neue Gesetz waren gemischt. (The reactions to the new law were mixed.)
details.usage: Used in everyday speech, chemistry ('chemische Reaktion'), and psychology; 'Reaktionszeit' (reaction time) is a standard compound.

details.etymology: From Old High German 'reht' (straight/right/just), cognate with Latin 'rectus' and English 'right'.
examples: Jeder hat das Recht auf freie Meinungsäußerung. (Everyone has the right to free expression.)
Sie studiert Recht an der Universität. (She studies law at the university.)
details.usage: Means both 'right' (entitlement) and 'law' (legal system); 'Menschenrecht' (human right) and 'Rechtsstaat' (rule of law) are key compounds.

details.etymology: From 'reich' (rich) with the suffix '-tum' (denoting a state or domain), from Old High German 'rihtuom'.
examples: Reichtum allein macht nicht glücklich. (Wealth alone does not make you happy.)
Der Reichtum an Kultur in dieser Stadt ist beeindruckend. (The richness of culture in this city is impressive.)
details.usage: Used for material wealth and figurative richness (e.g., 'Artenreichtum' = species richness); contrasted with 'Armut' (poverty).

details.etymology: Compound of 'Rück-' (back) and 'Sicht' (sight/view), originally meaning looking back or considering others.
examples: Bitte nehmen Sie Rücksicht auf andere Gäste. (Please be considerate of other guests.)
Er handelt ohne Rücksicht auf Verluste. (He acts without regard for losses.)
details.usage: Most commonly used in the phrase 'Rücksicht nehmen auf' (to be considerate of); 'rücksichtsvoll' (considerate) and 'rücksichtslos' (ruthless) are key adjectives.

details.etymology: From Old High German 'sin' (sense/mind/direction), cognate with English 'sense' via the shared Indo-European root.
examples: Was ist der Sinn des Lebens? (What is the meaning of life?)
Das ergibt keinen Sinn. (That makes no sense.)
details.usage: Has multiple meanings: sense (physical), meaning, and purpose; compounds include 'Wahnsinn' (madness), 'Blödsinn' (nonsense), 'sinnvoll' (meaningful).

details.etymology: From 'stimmen' (to tune/be correct), related to 'Stimme' (voice), originally referring to musical tuning and then mood.
examples: Die Stimmung auf der Party war ausgezeichnet. (The mood at the party was excellent.)
Er ist in guter Stimmung. (He is in a good mood.)
details.usage: Used for mood, atmosphere, and sentiment; 'Stimmungswechsel' (mood swing) and 'stimmungsvoll' (atmospheric) are common derivatives.

details.etymology: Compound of 'Tat' (deed/act) and 'Sache' (thing/matter), calqued from Latin 'res facti' (matter of fact).
examples: Das ist eine unbestreitbare Tatsache. (That is an indisputable fact.)
Wir müssen den Tatsachen ins Auge sehen. (We must face the facts.)
details.usage: A formal word for 'fact'; 'tatsächlich' (actually/indeed) is one of the most common German adverbs.

details.etymology: Borrowed from Latin 'tendentia', from 'tendere' (to stretch/tend toward).
examples: Es gibt eine Tendenz zu höheren Preisen. (There is a tendency toward higher prices.)
Die Tendenz ist steigend. (The trend is upward.)
details.usage: Used in economic, social, and statistical contexts; similar to 'Trend' but slightly more formal.

details.etymology: Compound of 'um' (around) and 'Fang' (from 'fangen', to catch/encompass), meaning what is encompassed or the scope.
examples: Der Umfang des Projekts ist enorm. (The scope of the project is enormous.)
Berechnen Sie den Umfang des Kreises. (Calculate the circumference of the circle.)
details.usage: Means both 'scope/extent' and 'circumference' (geometry); 'umfangreich' (extensive/comprehensive) is the common adjective.

details.etymology: Compound of 'um' (around) and 'Stand' (state/position), calqued from Latin 'circumstantia' (surrounding conditions).
examples: Unter diesen Umständen können wir nicht zustimmen. (Under these circumstances, we cannot agree.)
Machen Sie sich keine Umstände! (Don't go to any trouble!)
details.usage: The plural 'Umstände' also colloquially means 'fuss/trouble'; 'unter Umständen' (under certain circumstances, possibly) is a very common phrase.

details.etymology: Compound of 'um' (around) and 'Welt' (world), coined in the early 19th century to mean the surrounding world or environment.
examples: Wir müssen die Umwelt schützen. (We must protect the environment.)
Die Umweltverschmutzung ist ein großes Problem. (Environmental pollution is a major problem.)
details.usage: One of the most important words in modern German discourse; 'Umweltschutz' (environmental protection) and 'umweltfreundlich' (eco-friendly) are ubiquitous.

details.etymology: Compound of 'ur-' (original/primal) and 'Sache' (thing/matter), meaning the original cause or root matter.
examples: Die Ursache des Unfalls wird untersucht. (The cause of the accident is being investigated.)
Keine Ursache! (No problem! / You're welcome!)
details.usage: The phrase 'Keine Ursache!' is a polite response to 'Danke' (thank you); 'Ursache und Wirkung' (cause and effect) is a standard pairing.

details.etymology: From 'verbinden' (to connect/bind together), combining 'ver-' and 'binden' (to bind).
examples: Die Verbindung zwischen den beiden Städten ist gut. (The connection between the two cities is good.)
Ich setze mich mit Ihnen in Verbindung. (I will get in touch with you.)
details.usage: Used for physical connections, chemical compounds, and interpersonal contact; 'Telefonverbindung' (phone connection) and 'Zugverbindung' (train connection) are common.

details.etymology: From 'verbrauchen' (to consume/use up), combining 'ver-' (prefix indicating completion) and 'brauchen' (to need/use).
examples: Der Verbrauch von Energie muss sinken. (Energy consumption must decrease.)
Dieses Auto hat einen niedrigen Verbrauch. (This car has low fuel consumption.)
details.usage: Common in discussions about energy and resources; 'Verbraucher' (consumer) and 'Verbraucherschutz' (consumer protection) are key derivatives.

details.etymology: From Middle High German 'verdaht', related to 'denken' (to think), meaning a thought directed against someone.
examples: Er steht unter Verdacht. (He is under suspicion.)
Der Verdacht hat sich bestätigt. (The suspicion was confirmed.)
details.usage: Common in legal and crime contexts; 'verdächtig' (suspicious) and 'Verdächtiger' (suspect) are essential derivatives.

details.etymology: From 'verfahren' (to proceed), combining 'ver-' and 'fahren' (to go/drive), meaning the way one proceeds.
examples: Das Verfahren wurde eingestellt. (The proceedings were discontinued.)
Wir müssen ein neues Verfahren entwickeln. (We need to develop a new method.)
details.usage: Means both 'procedure/method' and 'legal proceedings'; 'Gerichtsverfahren' (court proceedings) and 'Herstellungsverfahren' (manufacturing process) are standard compounds.

details.etymology: From Middle High German 'verlust', related to 'verlieren' (to lose), from Old High German 'firliosan'.
examples: Der Verlust seines Vaters war ein schwerer Schlag. (The loss of his father was a heavy blow.)
Das Unternehmen machte große Verluste. (The company made large losses.)
details.usage: Used for personal loss and financial losses; 'Verlustangst' (fear of loss) is a psychological compound frequently discussed in German.

details.etymology: From 'versuchen' (to try/attempt), related to 'suchen' (to search), originally meaning to test or seek out.
examples: Der Versuch ist fehlgeschlagen. (The attempt failed.)
Im zweiten Versuch hat es geklappt. (It worked on the second try.)
details.usage: Means both 'attempt' and 'experiment' (scientific); 'Tierversuch' (animal experiment) is a frequently debated topic in Germany.

details.etymology: From 'vertrauen' (to trust), combining 'ver-' and 'trauen' (to trust/dare), from Old High German 'truwen'.
examples: Vertrauen muss man sich verdienen. (Trust must be earned.)
Ich habe volles Vertrauen in dich. (I have complete trust in you.)
details.usage: Central to German social and political life; 'Vertrauensfrage' (vote of confidence) is a key parliamentary term, and 'vertrauenswürdig' (trustworthy) is a common adjective.

details.etymology: From 'voraussetzen' (to presuppose), combining 'voraus' (ahead/in advance) and 'setzen' (to set).
examples: Gute Deutschkenntnisse sind Voraussetzung für den Job. (Good German skills are a prerequisite for the job.)
Unter der Voraussetzung, dass alle zustimmen, können wir beginnen. (On the condition that everyone agrees, we can begin.)
details.usage: Very common in job postings and academic requirements; often used in the phrase 'unter der Voraussetzung, dass...' (on the condition that...).

details.etymology: Compound of 'vor' (before/in front of) and 'Bild' (image/picture), meaning an image set before someone to emulate.
examples: Sie ist ein Vorbild für viele junge Frauen. (She is a role model for many young women.)
Eltern sollten ein gutes Vorbild sein. (Parents should be a good role model.)
details.usage: Used to describe someone worthy of emulation; 'vorbildlich' (exemplary) is the derived adjective.

details.etymology: Compound of 'vor' (before/pre-) and 'Urteil' (judgment), calqued from Latin 'praeiudicium' (prejudgment).
examples: Vorurteile sind schwer zu überwinden. (Prejudices are hard to overcome.)
Man sollte ohne Vorurteile auf andere zugehen. (One should approach others without prejudice.)
details.usage: A key word in social discourse; 'vorurteilsfrei' (free of prejudice) is the desired ideal in German public life.

details.etymology: From Old High German 'wantal' (change), related to 'wandeln' (to change/transform) and 'wenden' (to turn).
examples: Der Wandel der Gesellschaft vollzieht sich langsam. (The transformation of society happens slowly.)
Der Klimawandel betrifft uns alle. (Climate change affects us all.)
details.usage: Often used in compounds like 'Klimawandel' (climate change), 'Strukturwandel' (structural change), and 'Wertewandel' (shift in values).

details.etymology: From Old High German 'werd' (worthy/valuable), cognate with English 'worth'.
examples: Der Wert dieses Hauses ist gestiegen. (The value of this house has increased.)
Welche Werte sind dir wichtig? (What values are important to you?)
details.usage: Means both monetary 'value' and moral 'values'; 'wertvoll' (valuable), 'wertlos' (worthless), and 'Mehrwert' (added value) are key derivatives.

details.etymology: Compound of 'Wette' (bet/wager) and 'Bewerb' (from 'bewerben', to apply/compete), meaning a competitive contest.
examples: Der Wettbewerb zwischen den Firmen ist hart. (The competition between the companies is fierce.)
Sie hat den Wettbewerb gewonnen. (She won the competition.)
details.usage: Used for both market competition and contests/tournaments; 'wettbewerbsfähig' (competitive) is a key adjective in business German.

details.etymology: Compound of 'wider' (against) and 'Spruch' (saying/verdict), meaning a statement against something.
examples: Er legte Widerspruch gegen das Urteil ein. (He filed an objection against the verdict.)
Das steht im Widerspruch zu seinen früheren Aussagen. (That contradicts his earlier statements.)
details.usage: Used both as 'contradiction' and legal 'objection'; 'widersprüchlich' (contradictory) is the common adjective.

details.etymology: From Old High German 'wirt' (host/innkeeper) and '-schaft', originally meaning the management of a household or inn.
examples: Die Wirtschaft wächst in diesem Jahr. (The economy is growing this year.)
Er studiert Wirtschaft an der Universität. (He studies economics at the university.)
details.usage: Means 'economy', 'economics', or historically 'inn/pub'; 'Volkswirtschaft' (national economy) and 'Wirtschaftskrise' (economic crisis) are fundamental compounds.

details.etymology: From Old High German 'wizzan' (to know), from the Indo-European root meaning 'to see/perceive', cognate with Latin 'videre'.
examples: Wissen ist Macht. (Knowledge is power.)
Mein Wissen über dieses Thema ist begrenzt. (My knowledge of this topic is limited.)
details.usage: The noun is formed from the verb 'wissen' (to know facts); 'Wissenschaft' (science) and 'wissenswert' (worth knowing) are important derivatives.

details.etymology: Compound of 'wohl' (well) and 'Stand' (state/condition), meaning a state of being well-off.
examples: Der Wohlstand in Deutschland ist hoch. (Prosperity in Germany is high.)
Wohlstand bedeutet nicht nur Geld. (Prosperity does not only mean money.)
details.usage: A key word in post-war German history ('Wirtschaftswunder' era); 'Wohlstandsgesellschaft' (affluent society) is a common compound.

details.etymology: From Old High German 'giziugo', related to 'ziehen' (to pull/draw), originally meaning one drawn to testify.
examples: Der Zeuge sagte vor Gericht aus. (The witness testified in court.)
Waren Sie Zeuge des Unfalls? (Were you a witness to the accident?)
details.usage: A weak masculine noun (den/dem Zeugen); 'Zeugenaussage' (witness testimony) and 'Augenzeuge' (eyewitness) are standard compounds.

details.etymology: From Middle High German 'zwec' (nail/peg/target), originally a nail placed at the center of a target, evolving to mean 'purpose'.
examples: Zu welchem Zweck brauchen Sie das? (For what purpose do you need that?)
Der Zweck heiligt die Mittel. (The end justifies the means.)
details.usage: The proverb 'Der Zweck heiligt die Mittel' is widely known; 'zwecklos' (pointless) and 'zweckmäßig' (practical/functional) are common adjectives.

details.etymology: From Old High German 'zwival', related to 'zwei' (two), suggesting being of two minds.
examples: Ich habe Zweifel an seiner Ehrlichkeit. (I have doubts about his honesty.)
Ohne Zweifel ist das die richtige Entscheidung. (Without a doubt, that is the right decision.)
details.usage: The phrase 'im Zweifel' (in case of doubt) is common in legal contexts; 'zweifelhaft' (doubtful) and 'zweifellos' (doubtless) are key derivatives.

details.etymology: Compound of 'ab' (off) and 'Schnitt' (cut), literally a piece cut off, meaning a section or segment.
examples: Lesen Sie bitte den nächsten Abschnitt. (Please read the next section.)
Dieser Abschnitt meines Lebens war schwierig. (This phase of my life was difficult.)
details.usage: Used for sections of text, time periods, and physical segments; 'Lebensabschnitt' (phase of life) and 'Streckenabschnitt' (stretch of road) are common compounds.

details.etymology: From 'anbauen' (to cultivate/build on), combining 'an' (on/at) and 'bauen' (to build).
examples: Der Anbau von Weizen ist hier verbreitet. (The cultivation of wheat is common here.)
Wir planen einen Anbau am Haus. (We are planning an extension to the house.)
details.usage: Has two distinct meanings: agricultural cultivation and a building extension; context determines which meaning applies.

details.etymology: From 'anerkennen' (to recognize/acknowledge), combining 'an' and 'erkennen' (to recognize).
examples: Er verdient Anerkennung für seine Arbeit. (He deserves recognition for his work.)
Die Anerkennung ausländischer Abschlüsse ist oft schwierig. (The recognition of foreign degrees is often difficult.)
details.usage: Important in immigration contexts regarding credential recognition; also used for general appreciation and acknowledgment.

details.etymology: From 'annehmen' (to accept/assume), combining 'an' (on) and 'nehmen' (to take).
examples: Die Annahme des Angebots erfolgte sofort. (The acceptance of the offer was immediate.)
Das basiert auf falschen Annahmen. (That is based on wrong assumptions.)
details.usage: Has two meanings: 'acceptance' and 'assumption'; context is key to distinguishing them.

details.etymology: Compound of 'an' (at) and 'Sicht' (sight/view), meaning a view directed at something, hence an opinion.
examples: Meiner Ansicht nach ist das falsch. (In my opinion, that is wrong.)
Die Ansicht der Berge war wunderschön. (The view of the mountains was beautiful.)
details.usage: Used for both 'opinion/view' and a visual 'view'; 'Ansichtskarte' (picture postcard) is a charming compound.

details.etymology: From 'anstrengen' (to strain/exert), combining 'an' and 'strengen' (to tighten), related to 'streng' (strict).
examples: Mit großer Anstrengung erreichte er das Ziel. (With great effort, he reached the goal.)
Die Anstrengungen haben sich gelohnt. (The efforts paid off.)
details.usage: Used for both physical and mental effort; 'anstrengend' (exhausting/tiring) is the very common adjective form.

details.etymology: From Old High German 'armuoti', derived from 'arm' (poor), with the suffix '-ut' (later '-mut') indicating a state.
examples: Viele Menschen leben in Armut. (Many people live in poverty.)
Die Armut in der Region nimmt zu. (Poverty in the region is increasing.)
details.usage: A key social policy term; 'Altersarmut' (old-age poverty) and 'Kinderarmut' (child poverty) are frequently discussed in German politics.

details.etymology: Borrowed from Latin 'aspectus' (a looking at), from 'aspicere' (to look at).
examples: Wir müssen alle Aspekte berücksichtigen. (We must consider all aspects.)
Ein wichtiger Aspekt wurde übersehen. (An important aspect was overlooked.)
details.usage: Very common in academic and professional discourse; often used with 'unter dem Aspekt' (from the perspective of).

details.etymology: Borrowed from New Latin 'atmosphaera', from Greek 'atmos' (vapor) and 'sphaira' (sphere).
examples: Die Atmosphäre im Restaurant war gemütlich. (The atmosphere in the restaurant was cozy.)
Die Erdatmosphäre schützt uns vor Strahlung. (The Earth's atmosphere protects us from radiation.)
details.usage: Used for both the scientific atmosphere (air layer) and the figurative mood of a place; 'atmosphärisch' (atmospheric) is the adjective.

details.etymology: From 'aufhalten' (to stay/stop), combining 'auf' (up/on) and 'halten' (to hold/stop).
examples: Sein Aufenthalt in Berlin dauerte drei Monate. (His stay in Berlin lasted three months.)
Sie brauchen eine Aufenthaltserlaubnis. (You need a residence permit.)
details.usage: Crucial in immigration law; 'Aufenthaltserlaubnis' (residence permit) and 'Aufenthaltsraum' (lounge/common room) are important compounds.

details.etymology: From 'aufnehmen' (to take up/record/admit), combining 'auf' (up) and 'nehmen' (to take).
examples: Die Aufnahme des Fotos gelang perfekt. (The photo turned out perfectly.)
Die Aufnahme in den Verein war unkompliziert. (The admission to the club was uncomplicated.)
details.usage: Highly polysemous: means recording, photograph, admission, or reception; 'Tonaufnahme' (sound recording) and 'Aufnahmegespräch' (admission interview) are examples.

details.etymology: Compound of 'aus' (out) and 'Druck' (press/print), meaning something pressed out or expressed.
examples: Sein Gesichtsausdruck verriet seine Gefühle. (His facial expression revealed his feelings.)
Das ist ein umgangssprachlicher Ausdruck. (That is a colloquial expression.)
details.usage: Means both 'expression' (facial, verbal) and 'printout' (computer); 'Gesichtsausdruck' (facial expression) and 'ausdrucksvoll' (expressive) are common.

details.etymology: From 'auskommen' related roots, combining 'aus' (out) and 'Kunft' (from 'kommen', to come), meaning information that comes out.
examples: Können Sie mir Auskunft geben? (Can you give me information?)
Die Auskunft am Bahnhof war sehr hilfreich. (The information desk at the station was very helpful.)
details.usage: Also refers to a telephone directory service or an information desk; 'Auskunftspflicht' (obligation to provide information) is a legal term.

details.etymology: From 'auswählen' (to select), combining 'aus' (out) and 'Wahl' (choice/selection).
examples: Die Auswahl an Produkten ist riesig. (The selection of products is huge.)
Treffen Sie bitte Ihre Auswahl. (Please make your selection.)
details.usage: Used in shopping, sports team selection, and general choice contexts; 'Nationalauswahl' is an older term for national team.

details.etymology: From 'belasten' (to burden/load), from 'Last' (load/burden), meaning the state of being burdened.
examples: Die psychische Belastung war enorm. (The psychological strain was enormous.)
Hohe Steuern sind eine Belastung für kleine Firmen. (High taxes are a burden on small companies.)
details.usage: Used for physical load, psychological stress, financial burden, and environmental pollution; 'Umweltbelastung' (environmental pollution) is a frequent compound.

details.etymology: From 'belohnen' (to reward), combining 'be-' and 'lohnen' (to be worthwhile), related to 'Lohn' (wage/reward).
examples: Als Belohnung bekam er ein Eis. (As a reward, he got an ice cream.)
Es wurde eine Belohnung für Hinweise ausgesetzt. (A reward was offered for tips.)
details.usage: Used in both everyday contexts (rewards for children) and criminal investigations (reward for information).

details.etymology: From 'beobachten' (to observe), combining 'be-' and 'ob-achten' (to watch over), related to 'Acht' (attention).
examples: Durch genaue Beobachtung fand er die Ursache. (Through careful observation, he found the cause.)
Der Patient steht unter Beobachtung. (The patient is under observation.)
details.usage: Key term in scientific method and medical practice; 'Beobachtungsgabe' (powers of observation) is a valued quality.

details.etymology: From 'beschweren' (to burden/complain), combining 'be-' and 'schwer' (heavy), meaning something that weighs on someone.
examples: Er reichte eine Beschwerde ein. (He filed a complaint.)
Sie hat Beschwerden im Rücken. (She has back complaints/pain.)
details.usage: Means both 'complaint' (formal) and 'ailment/physical complaint' (medical); 'Beschwerdestelle' (complaints office) handles formal grievances.

details.etymology: From 'bestehen' (to exist/endure), combining 'be-' and 'Stand' (state), meaning what exists or endures.
examples: Der Bestand an Büchern in der Bibliothek ist groß. (The stock of books in the library is large.)
Der Bestand der Art ist bedroht. (The species' population is threatened.)
details.usage: Used for inventory, stock, and population of species; 'Bestandteil' (component) and 'Lagerbestand' (warehouse inventory) are common compounds.

details.etymology: From 'bestimmen' (to determine/designate), combining 'be-' and 'stimmen' (to tune/agree).
examples: Er hat seine Bestimmung gefunden. (He found his purpose/calling.)
Die gesetzlichen Bestimmungen sind klar. (The legal regulations are clear.)
details.usage: Means 'purpose/destiny', 'regulation', or 'determination'; the multiple meanings make context essential for understanding.

details.etymology: From 'betreuen' (to care for/look after), combining 'be-' and 'treuen' (related to 'treu', loyal/faithful).
examples: Die Betreuung der Kinder ist gut organisiert. (The childcare is well organized.)
Er braucht professionelle Betreuung. (He needs professional care.)
details.usage: Covers childcare, eldercare, and supervision; 'Kinderbetreuung' (childcare) is a major social policy topic in Germany.

details.etymology: From 'beweisen' (to prove), combining 'be-' and 'weisen' (to show/point), meaning something shown as proof.
examples: Haben Sie einen Beweis für Ihre Behauptung? (Do you have proof of your claim?)
Die Beweise gegen ihn sind erdrückend. (The evidence against him is overwhelming.)
details.usage: Central to legal and scientific language; 'Beweismittel' (piece of evidence) and 'beweisen' (to prove) are essential terms.

details.etymology: From 'bewusst' (conscious/aware), related to 'wissen' (to know), with '-sein' (being), meaning the state of being aware.
examples: Er verlor das Bewusstsein. (He lost consciousness.)
Das Umweltbewusstsein ist gestiegen. (Environmental awareness has increased.)
details.usage: Means both 'consciousness' (medical) and 'awareness'; 'Selbstbewusstsein' (self-confidence), 'Umweltbewusstsein' (environmental awareness) are key compounds.

details.etymology: Borrowed from French 'branche' (branch), ultimately from Late Latin 'branca' (paw/claw).
examples: In welcher Branche arbeiten Sie? (In which industry do you work?)
Die IT-Branche wächst schnell. (The IT industry is growing fast.)
details.usage: The standard word for 'industry/sector' in business German; 'branchenüblich' (standard in the industry) is a common adjective.

details.etymology: Borrowed from Latin 'chaos', from Greek 'khaos' (gaping void/abyss), originally referring to primordial emptiness.
examples: Nach dem Sturm herrschte Chaos. (After the storm, chaos reigned.)
Auf seinem Schreibtisch herrscht totales Chaos. (There is total chaos on his desk.)
details.usage: Pronounced with a hard 'K' sound in German; 'chaotisch' (chaotic) is the common adjective, and it is used casually for any messy situation.

details.etymology: Borrowed from French 'democratie', from Greek 'demokratia' (rule of the people), combining 'demos' (people) and 'kratos' (power).
examples: Deutschland ist eine parlamentarische Demokratie. (Germany is a parliamentary democracy.)
Die Demokratie muss verteidigt werden. (Democracy must be defended.)
details.usage: A cornerstone of German political vocabulary since 1949; 'demokratisch' (democratic) and 'Demokratieverständnis' (understanding of democracy) are important derivatives.

details.etymology: Compound of 'Dieb' (thief) and '-stahl' (from 'stehlen', to steal), from Old High German 'diubstal'.
examples: Er wurde wegen Diebstahls verurteilt. (He was convicted of theft.)
Der Diebstahl wurde sofort gemeldet. (The theft was reported immediately.)
details.usage: A legal term; 'Ladendiebstahl' (shoplifting) and 'Datendiebstahl' (data theft) are common modern compounds.

details.etymology: Compound of 'durch' (through) and 'Schnitt' (cut), meaning a cross-section, later evolving to 'average'.
examples: Im Durchschnitt arbeitet er acht Stunden am Tag. (On average, he works eight hours a day.)
Sein Gehalt liegt über dem Durchschnitt. (His salary is above average.)
details.usage: The phrase 'im Durchschnitt' (on average) is extremely common; 'durchschnittlich' (average/on average) is the standard adjective/adverb.

details.etymology: From Old High German 'era' (honor/respect), cognate with Latin 'honor' in concept though not in root.
examples: Es ist mir eine Ehre, Sie kennenzulernen. (It is an honor to meet you.)
Er verteidigte seine Ehre. (He defended his honor.)
details.usage: Used in formal phrases like 'zu Ehren von' (in honor of); 'Ehrenamt' (voluntary office/civic duty) is an important concept in German society.

details.etymology: From 'einigen' (to unite/agree), derived from 'ein' (one), meaning the process of becoming one or reaching agreement.
examples: Die Einigung wurde nach langen Verhandlungen erzielt. (The agreement was reached after long negotiations.)
Die deutsche Einigung von 1990 war historisch. (German unification in 1990 was historic.)
details.usage: Means both 'agreement' and 'unification'; 'Wiedervereinigung' (reunification) refers specifically to German reunification in 1990.

details.etymology: From 'ermitteln' (to investigate/determine), combining 'er-' and 'mitteln' (related to 'Mitte', middle/means).
examples: Die Ermittlungen dauern noch an. (The investigations are still ongoing.)
Die Polizei hat die Ermittlungen aufgenommen. (The police have taken up the investigation.)
details.usage: Primarily a legal/police term; 'Ermittlungsverfahren' (investigation proceedings) and 'Ermittler' (investigator) are standard compounds.

details.etymology: From 'ersetzen' (to replace), combining 'er-' and 'setzen' (to set/place), meaning something set in place of another.
examples: Wir brauchen einen Ersatz für den defekten Teil. (We need a replacement for the defective part.)
Kaffee ist kein Ersatz für Schlaf. (Coffee is no substitute for sleep.)
details.usage: Very productive in compounds: 'Ersatzteil' (spare part), 'Ersatzspieler' (substitute player), 'Ersatzreifen' (spare tire).

details.etymology: From 'erwarten' (to expect), combining 'er-' and 'warten' (to wait), meaning what one waits for.
examples: Die Erwartungen an ihn sind hoch. (The expectations of him are high.)
Das Ergebnis hat alle Erwartungen übertroffen. (The result exceeded all expectations.)
details.usage: Common in phrases like 'Erwartungen erfüllen' (to meet expectations) and 'wider Erwarten' (contrary to expectations).

details.etymology: From Old High German 'fluht', related to 'fliehen' (to flee), from the Indo-European root meaning to flow or flee.
examples: Die Flucht vor dem Krieg war gefährlich. (The flight from the war was dangerous.)
Er ergriff die Flucht. (He took flight/fled.)
details.usage: A highly relevant word in German public discourse regarding refugees; 'Flüchtling' (refugee) and 'Fluchtursache' (root cause of flight) are key related words.

details.etymology: From Old High German 'folga', related to 'folgen' (to follow), meaning what follows as a consequence.
examples: Die Folgen des Klimawandels sind gravierend. (The consequences of climate change are severe.)
Haben Sie die letzte Folge der Serie gesehen? (Did you see the last episode of the series?)
details.usage: Means both 'consequence' and 'episode' (of a series); 'infolge' (as a result of) and 'Folgekosten' (consequential costs) are common.

details.etymology: From Old High German 'frist' (period of time), related to 'First' (ridge), suggesting a fixed point in time.
examples: Die Frist endet am Freitag. (The deadline ends on Friday.)
Bitte halten Sie die Frist ein. (Please meet the deadline.)
details.usage: Very common in administrative and legal contexts; 'fristlos' (without notice, as in termination) and 'Kündigungsfrist' (notice period) are important compounds.

details.etymology: From Middle High German 'gebiet', related to 'gebieten' (to command), originally meaning a territory under one's command.
examples: Er ist Experte auf diesem Gebiet. (He is an expert in this field.)
Das Gebiet ist für Besucher gesperrt. (The area is closed to visitors.)
details.usage: Means both physical 'territory/area' and figurative 'field/domain'; 'Stadtgebiet' (city area) and 'Fachgebiet' (field of expertise) are common.

details.etymology: From 'geheim' (secret), derived from Old High German 'gaheim' (belonging to the home), with '-nis' forming a noun.
examples: Sie kann kein Geheimnis bewahren. (She cannot keep a secret.)
Das Geheimnis wurde gelüftet. (The secret was revealed.)
details.usage: Used for personal secrets and mysteries; 'Geschäftsgeheimnis' (trade secret) and 'geheimnisvoll' (mysterious) are important derivatives.

details.etymology: From Old High German 'gimeinida' (community), related to 'gemein' (common/shared), meaning a group sharing something in common.
examples: Die Gemeinde hat 5000 Einwohner. (The municipality has 5,000 inhabitants.)
Die Kirchengemeinde organisiert ein Fest. (The parish is organizing a celebration.)
details.usage: Means both 'municipality' (political unit) and 'parish/congregation' (religious); 'Gemeindeverwaltung' (municipal administration) is a common compound.

details.etymology: Borrowed from Latin 'generatio' (begetting/generation), from 'generare' (to beget).
examples: Jede Generation hat ihre eigenen Herausforderungen. (Every generation has its own challenges.)
Dieses Problem betrifft kommende Generationen. (This problem affects future generations.)
details.usage: Widely used in sociology and marketing; 'Generationenkonflikt' (generational conflict) and 'Generationenwechsel' (generational change) are standard compounds.

details.etymology: From 'gerecht' (just/fair), derived from 'Recht' (right/law), with '-igkeit' forming an abstract noun.
examples: Gerechtigkeit ist ein wichtiger Wert. (Justice is an important value.)
Er kämpft für soziale Gerechtigkeit. (He fights for social justice.)
details.usage: Central to political and ethical discourse; 'Chancengerechtigkeit' (equal opportunity) and 'Steuergerechtigkeit' (tax fairness) are debated topics.

details.etymology: From Old High German 'gislahti' (lineage/race), related to 'schlagen' (to strike), originally meaning a line of descent.
examples: Im Deutschen hat jedes Nomen ein grammatisches Geschlecht. (In German, every noun has a grammatical gender.)
Gleichberechtigung der Geschlechter ist wichtig. (Gender equality is important.)
details.usage: Means both 'gender/sex' and 'grammatical gender'; 'Geschlechterrolle' (gender role) is a modern social topic.

details.etymology: From 'gestalten' (to design/shape), related to 'Gestalt' (form/figure), from Old High German 'gistalt'.
examples: Die Gestaltung der Website ist modern. (The design of the website is modern.)
Kreative Gestaltung ist gefragt. (Creative design is in demand.)
details.usage: Used in art, design, and urban planning; 'Raumgestaltung' (interior design) and 'Arbeitsgestaltung' (work design) are common compounds.

details.etymology: From 'halten' (to hold/keep), meaning the way one holds oneself, both physically and morally.
examples: Seine Haltung zu diesem Thema ist klar. (His stance on this topic is clear.)
Eine gute Haltung ist wichtig für den Rücken. (Good posture is important for the back.)
details.usage: Means 'posture', 'attitude', and 'keeping' (of animals); 'Körperhaltung' (body posture) and 'Tierhaltung' (animal husbandry) are distinct compounds.

details.etymology: From Old High German 'hantal' (handling/dealing), related to 'Hand' (hand) and 'handeln' (to act/trade).
examples: Der Handel zwischen den Ländern blüht. (Trade between the countries is flourishing.)
Der Handel mit Drogen ist illegal. (The trade in drugs is illegal.)
details.usage: A fundamental economic term; 'Einzelhandel' (retail), 'Großhandel' (wholesale), and 'Freihandel' (free trade) are essential compounds.

details.etymology: From 'handeln' (to act), derived from 'Hand' (hand), with '-ung' suffix, meaning an act or action.
examples: Die Handlung des Films war spannend. (The plot of the film was exciting.)
Das war eine strafbare Handlung. (That was a punishable act.)
details.usage: Means both 'action/act' (legal, general) and 'plot' (of a story); 'Buchhandlung' (bookshop) is a common but semantically different compound.

details.etymology: Compound of 'hin' (toward) and 'Sicht' (sight/view), meaning a view directed toward a specific point.
examples: In dieser Hinsicht stimme ich zu. (In this respect, I agree.)
In mancher Hinsicht hat er recht. (In some respects, he is right.)
details.usage: Almost always used in the phrases 'in dieser Hinsicht' (in this respect) or 'in mancher Hinsicht' (in some respects).

details.etymology: Borrowed from French 'carriere' (career/racecourse), ultimately from Latin 'carrus' (wagon), via the idea of a road for vehicles.
examples: Sie hat eine erfolgreiche Karriere gemacht. (She had a successful career.)
Karriere und Familie zu vereinen ist schwer. (Combining career and family is difficult.)
details.usage: Often used in the phrase 'Karriere machen' (to build a career); 'Karriereleiter' (career ladder) is a common metaphor.

details.etymology: Borrowed from Greek 'katastrophe' (overturning), from 'kata-' (down) and 'strephein' (to turn).
examples: Das Erdbeben war eine Katastrophe. (The earthquake was a catastrophe.)
Die Prüfung war eine Katastrophe. (The exam was a disaster.)
details.usage: Used for both genuine disasters and colloquially for any terrible situation; 'Naturkatastrophe' (natural disaster) and 'katastrophal' (catastrophic) are common.

details.etymology: Borrowed from Latin 'crisis', from Greek 'krisis' (decision/turning point), related to 'krinein' (to judge/separate).
examples: Das Land steckt in einer Wirtschaftskrise. (The country is in an economic crisis.)
Jede Krise ist auch eine Chance. (Every crisis is also an opportunity.)
details.usage: Extremely common in modern discourse; 'Finanzkrise' (financial crisis), 'Flüchtlingskrise' (refugee crisis), and 'Midlife-Crisis' are all widely used.

details.etymology: From Old High German 'laga', related to 'liegen' (to lie), meaning the way something lies or is situated.
examples: Die Lage ist ernst. (The situation is serious.)
Das Hotel hat eine gute Lage. (The hotel has a good location.)
details.usage: Means both 'situation' and 'location/position'; 'in der Lage sein' (to be able to/in a position to) is a very common phrase.

details.etymology: From Middle High German 'lune', borrowed from Latin 'luna' (moon), reflecting the old belief that the moon affects mood.
examples: Er ist heute in guter Laune. (He is in a good mood today.)
Ihre Launen wechseln schnell. (Her moods change quickly.)
details.usage: Used in phrases like 'gute/schlechte Laune haben' (to be in a good/bad mood); 'launisch' (moody) is the adjective.

details.etymology: From Old High German 'lugi', related to 'lügen' (to lie), from a Germanic root meaning 'falsehood'.
examples: Das ist eine Lüge! (That is a lie!)
Lügen haben kurze Beine. (Lies have short legs — meaning lies are quickly found out.)
details.usage: The proverb 'Lügen haben kurze Beine' is very well known; 'Lügner' (liar) and 'Notlüge' (white lie) are common related words.

details.etymology: From Old High German 'maht', related to 'mögen' (to be able to) and 'machen' (to make), cognate with English 'might'.
examples: Wissen ist Macht. (Knowledge is power.)
Die Großmächte verhandeln über Frieden. (The great powers are negotiating peace.)
details.usage: A key political term; 'Großmacht' (great power), 'Machtmissbrauch' (abuse of power), and 'machtlos' (powerless) are standard compounds.

details.etymology: Borrowed from Latin 'massa' (lump/dough), from Greek 'maza' (barley cake), originally referring to a lump of matter.
examples: Die Masse des Objekts beträgt fünf Kilogramm. (The mass of the object is five kilograms.)
Eine große Masse an Menschen war versammelt. (A large mass of people was gathered.)
details.usage: Used in physics (mass), for large quantities, and for 'the masses' (common people); 'Massenmedien' (mass media) and 'Massenproduktion' (mass production) are key compounds.

details.etymology: Borrowed from Latin 'methodus', from Greek 'methodos' (pursuit of knowledge), combining 'meta' (after) and 'hodos' (way).
examples: Diese Methode ist sehr effektiv. (This method is very effective.)
Welche Methoden verwenden Sie? (Which methods do you use?)
details.usage: Standard in academic and professional contexts; 'Lehrmethode' (teaching method) and 'methodisch' (methodical) are common derivatives.

details.etymology: Borrowed from Latin 'minister' (servant/attendant), from 'minus' (less), originally meaning a subordinate servant of the state.
examples: Der Minister hielt eine Rede. (The minister gave a speech.)
Sie wurde zur Ministerin ernannt. (She was appointed minister.)
details.usage: The feminine form is 'Ministerin'; 'Ministerpräsident' (minister-president, equivalent to state premier) and 'Außenminister' (foreign minister) are key compounds.

details.etymology: Compound of 'miss-' (wrong/bad) and 'Brauch' (use/custom), meaning wrong or harmful use.
examples: Der Missbrauch von Drogen ist gefährlich. (The abuse of drugs is dangerous.)
Missbrauch von Macht muss verhindert werden. (Abuse of power must be prevented.)
details.usage: A serious term used in legal, medical, and social contexts; 'Machtmissbrauch' (abuse of power) and 'missbrauchen' (to abuse) are important derivatives.

details.etymology: Borrowed from French 'niveau' (level), from Latin 'libella' via Old French 'livel', meaning a horizontal level.
examples: Das Niveau des Kurses ist hoch. (The level of the course is high.)
Die Diskussion war auf niedrigem Niveau. (The discussion was at a low level.)
details.usage: Pronounced in the French manner; commonly used with 'Sprachniveau' (language level, as in A1-C2) and 'niveauvoll' (sophisticated).

details.etymology: From Old High German 'opfar', borrowed from Latin 'offerre' (to offer), originally meaning a religious offering or sacrifice.
examples: Die Opfer des Unfalls wurden ins Krankenhaus gebracht. (The victims of the accident were taken to the hospital.)
Er brachte große Opfer für seine Familie. (He made great sacrifices for his family.)
details.usage: Means both 'victim' and 'sacrifice'; in youth slang 'Opfer' is used as an insult meaning 'loser', which is considered offensive.

details.etymology: Borrowed from French 'partie' (part/party), from Latin 'pars' (part), meaning a group sharing a common position.
examples: Er ist Mitglied einer politischen Partei. (He is a member of a political party.)
Beide Parteien müssen Kompromisse eingehen. (Both parties must make compromises.)
details.usage: Used for political parties and parties in a legal dispute; 'Parteiensystem' (party system) and 'parteiisch' (biased) are standard terms.

details.etymology: Borrowed from Latin 'phasis', from Greek 'phasis' (appearance), originally referring to phases of the moon.
examples: Er durchlebt gerade eine schwierige Phase. (He is going through a difficult phase right now.)
Das Projekt befindet sich in der letzten Phase. (The project is in its final phase.)
details.usage: Used in science, psychology, and everyday language for stages or periods; 'Übergangsphase' (transitional phase) is a common compound.

details.etymology: Borrowed from Latin 'protestari' (to declare publicly), combining 'pro' (forth) and 'testari' (to testify).
examples: Die Bürger gingen aus Protest auf die Straße. (The citizens took to the streets in protest.)
Sein Protest blieb ohne Wirkung. (His protest had no effect.)
details.usage: Common in political discourse; 'Protestbewegung' (protest movement), 'Protestwähler' (protest voter), and 'protestieren' (to protest) are widely used.

details.etymology: Borrowed from Latin 'publicum' (the public/community), from 'publicus' (of the people).
examples: Das Publikum applaudierte begeistert. (The audience applauded enthusiastically.)
Das Buch richtet sich an ein breites Publikum. (The book is aimed at a broad audience.)
details.usage: Used for theater audiences, readership, and the general public; 'Fachpublikum' (specialist audience) and 'Publikumsverkehr' (public access) are standard compounds.

details.etymology: From Old High German 'quella', related to 'quellen' (to well up/spring forth), originally meaning a spring of water.
examples: Wikipedia ist nicht immer eine zuverlässige Quelle. (Wikipedia is not always a reliable source.)
Die Quelle des Flusses liegt in den Bergen. (The source of the river is in the mountains.)
details.usage: Used for both water sources and information sources; 'Quellenangabe' (source citation) is essential in academic writing.

details.etymology: From Old High German 'rama' (frame/support), related to Gothic 'ramjan' (to support).
examples: Im Rahmen des Projekts wurde viel erreicht. (A lot was achieved within the framework of the project.)
Das Bild braucht einen neuen Rahmen. (The picture needs a new frame.)
details.usage: Very common in the phrase 'im Rahmen von' (within the framework of/as part of); 'Rahmenbedingungen' (framework conditions) is a standard compound.

details.etymology: Compound of 'Rat' (advice/counsel) and 'Schlag' (stroke/hit), originally meaning a stroke or decision of a council.
examples: Hast du einen Ratschlag für mich? (Do you have some advice for me?)
Seine Ratschläge sind immer hilfreich. (His advice is always helpful.)
details.usage: More formal than simply 'Rat' (advice); 'Ratgeber' (advisor/guidebook) is a related and very common word.

details.etymology: Borrowed from Latin 'reformare' (to form again/reshape), combining 're-' (again) and 'formare' (to form).
examples: Die Regierung plant eine Reform des Gesundheitssystems. (The government is planning a reform of the health system.)
Reformen brauchen Zeit. (Reforms take time.)
details.usage: A key political word; 'Steuerreform' (tax reform), 'Bildungsreform' (education reform), and 'reformbedürftig' (in need of reform) are common.

details.etymology: Compound of 'Reihe' (row/series) and 'Folge' (sequence/order), meaning the order in which things follow in a series.
examples: Bitte beachten Sie die richtige Reihenfolge. (Please note the correct order.)
Die Reihenfolge der Aufgaben ist wichtig. (The order of the tasks is important.)
details.usage: A very practical everyday word; 'in alphabetischer Reihenfolge' (in alphabetical order) is a standard phrase.

details.etymology: Borrowed from Latin 'religio' (reverence/obligation), possibly from 'relegere' (to re-read) or 'religare' (to bind back).
examples: Religionsfreiheit ist ein Grundrecht. (Freedom of religion is a fundamental right.)
In Deutschland gibt es viele verschiedene Religionen. (There are many different religions in Germany.)
details.usage: A protected right under Germany's Basic Law; 'Religionsunterricht' (religious education) is a school subject in most German states.

details.etymology: Borrowed from French 'rente' (income/annuity), from Latin 'reddita' (things returned), from 'reddere' (to give back).
examples: Er geht nächstes Jahr in Rente. (He is retiring next year.)
Die Renten müssen steigen. (Pensions must increase.)
details.usage: Means both 'pension' and 'retirement'; 'Rentenversicherung' (pension insurance) and 'Rentner' (retiree) are essential compounds in German social policy.

details.etymology: Borrowed from Italian 'risico/rischio', possibly from Arabic 'rizq' (fortune/livelihood) or Greek 'rhizikon' (root/cliff danger).
examples: Das Risiko ist zu hoch. (The risk is too high.)
Er scheut kein Risiko. (He shies away from no risk.)
details.usage: Very common in finance and insurance; 'Risikobereitschaft' (willingness to take risks) and 'risikoreich' (risky) are standard compounds.

details.etymology: From Old High German 'ruof', related to 'rufen' (to call), meaning a call or, by extension, one's reputation (what is said about someone).
examples: Die Firma hat einen guten Ruf. (The company has a good reputation.)
Ein lauter Ruf hallte durch den Wald. (A loud call echoed through the forest.)
details.usage: Means both 'call/shout' and 'reputation'; 'Berufung' (vocation/appeal) and 'Notruf' (emergency call) are important related words.

details.etymology: From Middle High German 'schicsal', related to 'schicken' (to send), originally meaning what is sent or ordained by fate.
examples: Das Schicksal hat es so gewollt. (Fate willed it so.)
Viele Flüchtlinge teilen ein ähnliches Schicksal. (Many refugees share a similar fate.)
details.usage: A culturally important word in German literature and philosophy; 'schicksalhaft' (fateful) and 'Schicksalsschlag' (stroke of fate) are common derivatives.

details.etymology: From 'schwach' (weak), derived from Old High German 'swah', with the suffix '-e' forming a noun meaning the state of being weak.
examples: Jeder hat seine Schwächen. (Everyone has their weaknesses.)
Er hat eine Schwäche für Schokolade. (He has a weakness for chocolate.)
details.usage: The phrase 'eine Schwäche haben für' (to have a weakness/soft spot for) is very common; opposite of 'Stärke' (strength).

details.etymology: From 'spannen' (to stretch/tighten), derived from Old High German 'spannan', meaning a state of being stretched or tense.
examples: Die Spannung im Film war unerträglich. (The suspense in the film was unbearable.)
Es gibt politische Spannungen zwischen den Ländern. (There are political tensions between the countries.)
details.usage: Means 'tension', 'suspense', and 'voltage' (electrical); 'spannend' (exciting/thrilling) is one of the most common German adjectives.

details.etymology: From 'stark' (strong), derived from Old High German 'starc', with the suffix '-e' forming a noun meaning strength.
examples: Geduld ist eine ihrer größten Stärken. (Patience is one of her greatest strengths.)
Die Stärke des Windes nimmt zu. (The strength of the wind is increasing.)
details.usage: Also means 'starch' (in cooking/chemistry); 'Stärken und Schwächen' (strengths and weaknesses) is a standard pair in job interviews.

details.etymology: From Old High German 'stolz' (proud/splendid), possibly related to 'Stelze' (stilt), suggesting standing tall.
examples: Er ist der Stolz seiner Familie. (He is the pride of his family.)
Mit Stolz präsentierte sie ihr Werk. (She presented her work with pride.)
details.usage: Both a noun (der Stolz = pride) and an adjective (stolz = proud); 'stolz auf etwas sein' (to be proud of something) is a key phrase.

details.etymology: Borrowed from French 'strategie', from Greek 'strategia' (generalship), combining 'stratos' (army) and 'agein' (to lead).
examples: Wir brauchen eine neue Strategie. (We need a new strategy.)
Die Strategie des Unternehmens war erfolgreich. (The company's strategy was successful.)
details.usage: Used in military, business, and everyday contexts; 'strategisch' (strategic) and 'Marketingstrategie' (marketing strategy) are standard terms.

details.etymology: Borrowed from Latin 'structura' (construction/arrangement), from 'struere' (to build/pile up).
examples: Die Struktur des Textes ist klar. (The structure of the text is clear.)
Wir müssen die Strukturen verändern. (We need to change the structures.)
details.usage: Used in many fields from architecture to sociology; 'strukturell' (structural) and 'Infrastruktur' (infrastructure) are key derivatives.

details.etymology: Borrowed from Latin 'symbolum', from Greek 'symbolon' (token/sign), originally meaning two halves of a broken object used for identification.
examples: Die Taube ist ein Symbol des Friedens. (The dove is a symbol of peace.)
Das Brandenburger Tor ist ein Symbol Berlins. (The Brandenburg Gate is a symbol of Berlin.)
details.usage: Used in literature, art, and everyday life; 'symbolisch' (symbolic) and 'Statussymbol' (status symbol) are common derivatives.

details.etymology: Borrowed from Greek 'techne' (art/skill/craft) via Latin 'technica', originally meaning practical skill or craftsmanship.
examples: Die Technik entwickelt sich rasant. (Technology is developing rapidly.)
Er beherrscht verschiedene Maltechniken. (He masters various painting techniques.)
details.usage: Means both 'technology' and 'technique'; Germany's 'Technische Universität' (TU, technical university) is a prestigious institution type.

details.etymology: From 'teilnehmen' (to participate), combining 'Teil' (part) and 'nehmen' (to take), meaning taking part.
examples: Die Teilnahme am Kurs ist freiwillig. (Participation in the course is voluntary.)
Vielen Dank für Ihre Teilnahme. (Thank you very much for your participation.)
details.usage: Common in formal contexts; also means 'sympathy/compassion' in the phrase 'herzliche Teilnahme' (heartfelt condolences); 'Teilnehmer' (participant) is the derived noun.

details.etymology: Borrowed from Latin 'theoria', from Greek 'theoria' (contemplation/speculation), from 'theorein' (to look at).
examples: In der Theorie klingt das gut. (In theory, that sounds good.)
Diese Theorie wurde widerlegt. (This theory was refuted.)
details.usage: Contrasted with 'Praxis' (practice) in the phrase 'Theorie und Praxis' (theory and practice); 'theoretisch' (theoretical) is the standard adjective.

details.etymology: Borrowed from Latin 'tolerantia' (endurance/patience), from 'tolerare' (to bear/endure).
examples: Toleranz gegenüber anderen Meinungen ist wichtig. (Tolerance of other opinions is important.)
Die Toleranz in der Gesellschaft wächst. (Tolerance in society is growing.)
details.usage: A key value in German civic education; 'tolerant' (tolerant) and 'Nulltoleranz' (zero tolerance) are widely used terms.

details.etymology: Borrowed from Latin 'traditio' (handing over/delivery), from 'tradere' (to hand over), combining 'trans' (across) and 'dare' (to give).
examples: Das ist eine alte Tradition in unserer Familie. (That is an old tradition in our family.)
Traditionen sollte man pflegen. (Traditions should be maintained.)
details.usage: Important in German cultural identity; 'traditionell' (traditional) and 'traditionsreich' (rich in tradition) are common adjectives.

details.etymology: Borrowed from English 'trend', originally meaning 'to turn/revolve', from Old English 'trendan'.
examples: Der Trend geht zu nachhaltiger Mode. (The trend is toward sustainable fashion.)
Dieses Produkt liegt voll im Trend. (This product is fully on trend.)
details.usage: A well-established anglicism in German; 'trendig' or 'trendy' (both used) and 'Gegentrend' (counter-trend) are common derivatives.

details.etymology: Compound of 'über' (over) and 'Blick' (view/glance), meaning a view from above, hence an overview.
examples: Können Sie mir einen Überblick geben? (Can you give me an overview?)
Er hat den Überblick verloren. (He lost track/overview.)
details.usage: Very common in professional and academic contexts; 'den Überblick behalten' (to keep track) and 'den Überblick verlieren' (to lose track) are standard phrases.

details.etymology: From 'umsetzen' (to convert/turn over), combining 'um' (around) and 'setzen' (to set), meaning the turnover of goods into money.
examples: Der Umsatz ist in diesem Quartal gestiegen. (Revenue increased this quarter.)
Das Unternehmen machte einen Umsatz von zehn Millionen Euro. (The company generated revenue of ten million euros.)
details.usage: A fundamental business term; 'Umsatzsteuer' (sales tax/VAT) and 'Umsatzwachstum' (revenue growth) are essential in German business language.

details.etymology: From 'unterstützen' (to support), combining 'unter' (under) and 'stützen' (to prop up/support).
