examples: Vielen Dank für Ihre Unterstützung. (Thank you very much for your support.)
Er braucht finanzielle Unterstützung. (He needs financial support.)
details.usage: Used in personal, financial, and political contexts; 'Unterstützungsleistung' (support benefit) is used in social services.

details.etymology: From 'verbessern' (to improve), combining 'ver-' and 'bessern' (to better), from 'besser' (better).
examples: Es gibt noch Raum für Verbesserungen. (There is still room for improvement.)
Die Verbesserung der Infrastruktur ist geplant. (The improvement of infrastructure is planned.)
details.usage: A standard word in quality management and everyday language; 'Verbesserungsvorschlag' (suggestion for improvement) is a common compound.

details.etymology: From 'vereinbaren' (to agree upon), combining 'ver-' and 'einbaren' (related to 'ein' = one), meaning to make one or reach agreement.
examples: Wir haben eine Vereinbarung getroffen. (We reached an agreement.)
Laut Vereinbarung beginnt das Projekt im Januar. (According to the agreement, the project starts in January.)
details.usage: Used in legal and business contexts for formal agreements; similar to 'Abkommen' (accord) but often used for smaller-scale agreements.

details.etymology: From 'verhandeln' (to negotiate), combining 'ver-' and 'handeln' (to act/trade), meaning the process of trading or dealing.
examples: Die Verhandlungen dauerten mehrere Stunden. (The negotiations lasted several hours.)
Die Gerichtsverhandlung findet morgen statt. (The court hearing takes place tomorrow.)
details.usage: Means both 'negotiation' and 'trial/hearing' (legal); 'Verhandlungsbasis' (basis for negotiation) and 'Friedensverhandlungen' (peace negotiations) are common.

details.etymology: From 'vermögen' (to be able to/have the power to), combining 'ver-' and 'mögen' (to be able), meaning what one is able to possess.
examples: Er hat ein großes Vermögen geerbt. (He inherited a large fortune.)
Das Vermögen der Familie wird auf Millionen geschätzt. (The family's wealth is estimated at millions.)
details.usage: Means 'wealth/fortune/assets' and also 'ability/capacity'; 'Vermögenssteuer' (wealth tax) and 'Leistungsvermögen' (capacity/performance ability) are distinct compound uses.

details.etymology: From 'verpflichten' (to obligate), combining 'ver-' and 'Pflicht' (duty), meaning the state of being bound to a duty.
examples: Er hat viele Verpflichtungen. (He has many obligations.)
Die Verpflichtung zur Geheimhaltung ist bindend. (The obligation of confidentiality is binding.)
details.usage: Used in legal, professional, and personal contexts; also used in sports for 'signing' a player: 'die Verpflichtung eines Spielers'.

details.etymology: From 'verwalten' (to administer/manage), combining 'ver-' and 'walten' (to govern/rule).
examples: Die Verwaltung des Gebäudes ist effizient. (The management of the building is efficient.)
Er arbeitet in der öffentlichen Verwaltung. (He works in public administration.)
details.usage: Central to German bureaucracy; 'Stadtverwaltung' (city administration) and 'Verwaltungskosten' (administrative costs) are ubiquitous compounds.

details.etymology: Compound of 'viel' (many/much) and 'Falt' (from 'falten', to fold, or '-falt' meaning '-fold'), meaning manifoldness.
examples: Die Vielfalt der Kulturen bereichert unser Land. (The diversity of cultures enriches our country.)
Deutschland zeichnet sich durch biologische Vielfalt aus. (Germany is characterized by biological diversity.)
details.usage: A key word in diversity discourse; 'Artenvielfalt' (biodiversity) and 'vielfältig' (diverse/varied) are important derivatives.

details.etymology: Compound of 'vor' (before/ahead) and 'Fahrt' (drive/journey), meaning the right to drive ahead.
examples: Er hat die Vorfahrt missachtet. (He failed to yield the right of way.)
An dieser Kreuzung haben Sie Vorfahrt. (You have the right of way at this intersection.)
details.usage: A crucial traffic term; 'Vorfahrt beachten' (yield) and 'Vorfahrtsstraße' (priority road) are essential for German driving.

details.etymology: From 'warnen' (to warn), from Old High German 'warnon' (to take heed), cognate with English 'warn'.
examples: Die Warnung kam zu spät. (The warning came too late.)
Es gibt eine Warnung vor starkem Regen. (There is a warning of heavy rain.)
details.usage: Used for official warnings and personal cautions; 'Sturmwarnung' (storm warning) and 'Warnhinweis' (warning notice) are common compounds.

details.etymology: From 'wenden' (to turn), from Old High German 'wenten', meaning a turning point or fundamental change.
examples: Die Wende von 1989 veränderte Deutschland. (The turning point of 1989 changed Germany.)
Das Spiel nahm eine überraschende Wende. (The game took a surprising turn.)
details.usage: In German history, 'die Wende' specifically refers to the peaceful revolution and fall of the Berlin Wall in 1989; also means 'turn' in sports and swimming.

details.etymology: Compound of 'wider' (against) and 'Stand' (standing/position), meaning standing against something.
examples: Der Widerstand gegen das neue Gesetz wächst. (Resistance to the new law is growing.)
Der elektrische Widerstand wird in Ohm gemessen. (Electrical resistance is measured in ohms.)
details.usage: Historically significant: 'der Widerstand' refers to the German resistance against the Nazi regime; also used in physics for electrical resistance.

details.etymology: Compound of 'Wissen' (knowledge) and '-schaft' (suffix denoting a collective or state), meaning the systematic pursuit of knowledge.
examples: Die Wissenschaft hat große Fortschritte gemacht. (Science has made great progress.)
Er widmet sein Leben der Wissenschaft. (He dedicates his life to science.)
details.usage: Broader than English 'science' as it includes humanities ('Geisteswissenschaften'); 'Wissenschaftler' (scientist/scholar) and 'wissenschaftlich' (scientific/academic) are key derivatives.

details.etymology: From Old High German 'wuntar' (wonder/miracle), of uncertain origin, possibly related to 'wünschen' (to wish).
examples: Es ist ein Wunder, dass niemand verletzt wurde. (It is a miracle that nobody was injured.)
Das Wirtschaftswunder veränderte Deutschland. (The economic miracle transformed Germany.)
details.usage: The 'Wirtschaftswunder' (economic miracle) of the 1950s-60s is a defining term in German history; 'wunderbar' (wonderful) and 'wunderschön' (gorgeous) are beloved adjectives.

details.etymology: Compound of 'zu' (to) and 'Fall' (fall/case), meaning something that falls to one, i.e., happens by chance.
examples: Was für ein Zufall! (What a coincidence!)
Durch Zufall habe ich ihn getroffen. (I met him by chance.)
details.usage: The phrase 'durch Zufall' (by chance) is very common; 'zufällig' (coincidental/by chance) is the standard adverb/adjective.

details.etymology: From 'zustimmen' (to agree/consent), combining 'zu' (to) and 'stimmen' (to vote/agree), meaning voicing agreement.
examples: Er nickte als Zeichen der Zustimmung. (He nodded as a sign of agreement.)
Ohne Ihre Zustimmung geht es nicht weiter. (Without your consent, we cannot proceed.)
details.usage: Used in legal and everyday contexts for consent/approval; 'Zustimmungspflicht' (requirement of consent) is an important legal term.

details.etymology: From 'abkommen' (to come to an agreement/deviate), combining 'ab' (off/from) and 'kommen' (to come).
examples: Die Länder haben ein Abkommen unterzeichnet. (The countries signed an agreement.)
Das Handelsabkommen tritt bald in Kraft. (The trade agreement will soon take effect.)
details.usage: Used for formal international agreements and treaties; 'Friedensabkommen' (peace agreement) and 'Handelsabkommen' (trade agreement) are common compounds.

details.etymology: Compound of 'all' (all/every) and 'Tag' (day), meaning every day or the routine of daily life.
examples: Der Alltag kann manchmal langweilig sein. (Everyday life can sometimes be boring.)
Im Alltag spreche ich meistens Deutsch. (In everyday life, I mostly speak German.)
details.usage: Very common in the phrase 'im Alltag' (in everyday life); 'alltäglich' (everyday/commonplace) and 'Arbeitsalltag' (daily work routine) are standard derivatives.

details.etymology: From 'aufwenden' (to spend/expend), combining 'auf' (up/on) and 'wenden' (to turn/apply).
examples: Der Aufwand hat sich gelohnt. (The effort was worth it.)
Mit wenig Aufwand kann man viel erreichen. (With little effort, one can achieve a lot.)
details.usage: Used for effort, expense, and expenditure; 'aufwendig' or 'aufwändig' (elaborate/costly) is the common adjective, and both spellings are accepted.

details.etymology: From 'ausgeben' (to spend/issue), combining 'aus' (out) and 'geben' (to give).
examples: Die Ausgaben sind höher als geplant. (The expenses are higher than planned.)
Die neueste Ausgabe der Zeitschrift ist erschienen. (The latest issue of the magazine has been published.)
details.usage: Means both 'expenditure/expense' (usually plural) and 'edition/issue' (of a publication); opposite of 'Einnahme' (income).

details.etymology: From 'behören' (an old verb meaning to belong to/be assigned to), related to 'gehören' (to belong).
examples: Die zuständige Behörde muss informiert werden. (The responsible authority must be informed.)
Er arbeitet bei einer Behörde. (He works at a government agency.)
details.usage: The standard word for government authority/agency; 'Behördengang' (visit to a government office) and 'Ausländerbehörde' (foreigners' registration office) are common compounds.

details.etymology: From 'bestätigen' (to confirm), combining 'be-' and 'stätigen' (related to 'stätig', steady), meaning to make steady or certain.
examples: Ich warte noch auf die Bestätigung. (I am still waiting for the confirmation.)
Bitte senden Sie mir eine schriftliche Bestätigung. (Please send me a written confirmation.)
details.usage: Very common in administrative and business communication; 'Buchungsbestätigung' (booking confirmation) and 'Auftragsbestätigung' (order confirmation) are standard compounds.

details.etymology: From 'beurteilen' (to assess/judge), combining 'be-' and 'urteilen' (to judge), from 'Urteil' (judgment).
examples: Die Beurteilung der Leistung ist fair. (The assessment of performance is fair.)
Er bekam eine gute Beurteilung von seinem Chef. (He received a good evaluation from his boss.)
details.usage: Used in professional and educational contexts for evaluations; 'Leistungsbeurteilung' (performance review) is a standard HR term.

details.etymology: From Old High German 'turen' (to last), related to Latin 'durare' (to harden/endure), meaning the span of time something lasts.
examples: Die Dauer des Kurses beträgt sechs Monate. (The duration of the course is six months.)
Auf Dauer ist das keine Lösung. (In the long run, that is not a solution.)
details.usage: The phrase 'auf Dauer' (in the long run) is very common; 'dauerhaft' (permanent/lasting) and 'Dauerauftrag' (standing order) are key derivatives.

details.etymology: Borrowed from French 'debat' (debate), from 'debattre' (to fight/discuss), combining 'de-' (down) and 'battre' (to beat).
examples: Die Debatte im Parlament war hitzig. (The debate in parliament was heated.)
Über dieses Thema gibt es eine lebhafte Debatte. (There is a lively debate about this topic.)
details.usage: Standard in political and public discourse; 'Bundestagsdebatte' (Bundestag debate) and 'debattieren' (to debate) are essential terms.

details.etymology: Compound of 'Eifer' (zeal/fervor) and 'Sucht' (addiction/obsession), meaning a zealous, obsessive desire to possess or protect.
examples: Eifersucht kann eine Beziehung zerstören. (Jealousy can destroy a relationship.)
Er wurde von Eifersucht geplagt. (He was plagued by jealousy.)
details.usage: A powerful emotional term; 'eifersüchtig' (jealous) is the adjective, and it is distinct from 'Neid' (envy), which relates to wanting what others have.

details.etymology: From 'einrichten' (to furnish/set up), combining 'ein' (in) and 'richten' (to direct/arrange).
examples: Die Einrichtung der Wohnung ist modern. (The furnishing of the apartment is modern.)
Es gibt viele soziale Einrichtungen in der Stadt. (There are many social institutions in the city.)
details.usage: Means both 'furnishing/decor' and 'institution/facility'; 'Bildungseinrichtung' (educational institution) and 'Pflegeeinrichtung' (care facility) are common compounds.

details.etymology: From 'einwenden' (to object), combining 'ein' (in) and 'wenden' (to turn), meaning to turn something in as an objection.
examples: Hat jemand einen Einwand? (Does anyone have an objection?)
Gegen den Plan gab es keine Einwände. (There were no objections to the plan.)
details.usage: Used in meetings, legal proceedings, and general discussion; 'einwandfrei' (flawless/impeccable) literally means 'without objection'.

details.etymology: Borrowed from French 'energie', from Latin 'energia', from Greek 'energeia' (activity/operation), combining 'en' (in) and 'ergon' (work).
examples: Erneuerbare Energien sind die Zukunft. (Renewable energies are the future.)
Ich habe heute keine Energie. (I have no energy today.)
details.usage: Central to Germany's 'Energiewende' (energy transition); 'Energiequelle' (energy source) and 'energieeffizient' (energy-efficient) are key compounds.

details.etymology: From 'erleichtern' (to relieve/ease), combining 'er-' and 'leichtern' (from 'leicht', light/easy), meaning to make lighter.
examples: Die Erleichterung war ihr anzusehen. (The relief was visible on her face.)
Das neue Gesetz bringt Erleichterungen für Familien. (The new law brings relief for families.)
details.usage: Means both emotional 'relief' and practical 'easing/facilitation'; often used in the phrase 'zu meiner Erleichterung' (to my relief).

details.etymology: From 'erscheinen' (to appear), combining 'er-' and 'scheinen' (to shine/seem), meaning an appearance or phenomenon.
examples: Das ist eine häufige Erscheinung. (That is a common phenomenon.)
Er machte eine beeindruckende Erscheinung. (He cut an impressive figure.)
details.usage: Means 'appearance', 'phenomenon', and 'publication' (of a book); 'Erscheinungsbild' (outward appearance) and 'Begleiterscheinung' (accompanying phenomenon) are standard compounds.

details.etymology: Borrowed from Latin 'existentia' (existence), from 'existere' (to step out/emerge), combining 'ex' (out) and 'sistere' (to stand).
examples: Die Existenz der Firma ist bedroht. (The company's existence is threatened.)
Er hat sich eine neue Existenz aufgebaut. (He built a new livelihood for himself.)
details.usage: Means both philosophical 'existence' and practical 'livelihood'; 'Existenzgründung' (starting a business) and 'existenziell' (existential) are important derivatives.

details.etymology: Borrowed from Latin 'phantasia', from Greek 'phantasia' (imagination/appearance), related to 'phainein' (to show).
examples: Kinder haben eine lebhafte Fantasie. (Children have a vivid imagination.)
Das übersteigt meine Fantasie. (That exceeds my imagination.)
details.usage: Also spelled 'Phantasie' (older spelling); 'fantasievoll' (imaginative) and 'Fantasiegebilde' (figment of imagination) are common derivatives.

details.etymology: Borrowed from Latin 'factor' (maker/doer), from 'facere' (to do/make), meaning something that contributes to a result.
examples: Zeit ist ein entscheidender Faktor. (Time is a decisive factor.)
Viele Faktoren spielen eine Rolle. (Many factors play a role.)
details.usage: Used in mathematics, science, and general analysis; 'Risikofaktor' (risk factor) and 'Erfolgsfaktor' (success factor) are common compounds.

details.etymology: Borrowed from Latin 'figura' (form/shape/figure), from 'fingere' (to shape/mold).
examples: Sie hat eine schlanke Figur. (She has a slim figure.)
Die Hauptfigur des Romans ist sehr interessant. (The main character of the novel is very interesting.)
details.usage: Means 'figure' (body shape), 'character' (in literature/film), and 'piece' (in chess); 'Spielfigur' (game piece) and 'Romanfigur' (novel character) are common.

details.etymology: Borrowed from Latin 'functio' (performance/execution), from 'fungi' (to perform/discharge).
examples: Welche Funktion hat dieser Knopf? (What function does this button have?)
Er übt die Funktion des Vorsitzenden aus. (He performs the function of chairman.)
details.usage: Used in technology, mathematics, and organizational contexts; 'funktionieren' (to function/work) and 'Fehlfunktion' (malfunction) are key derivatives.

details.etymology: Compound of 'gegen' (against/opposite) and 'Stand' (standing), calqued from Latin 'objectum' (something thrown before).
examples: Was ist das für ein Gegenstand? (What kind of object is that?)
Das ist der Gegenstand unserer Diskussion. (That is the subject of our discussion.)
details.usage: Means both a physical 'object' and a 'subject/topic' (of discussion); 'gegenstandslos' (irrelevant/void) is an important adjective.

details.etymology: From 'Land' (land), with the prefix 'ge-' forming a collective, meaning a stretch of terrain or grounds.
examples: Das Gelände der Fabrik ist eingezäunt. (The factory grounds are fenced in.)
Das Gelände ist hügelig. (The terrain is hilly.)
details.usage: Means both 'terrain' and 'grounds/premises'; 'Geländewagen' (off-road vehicle/SUV) and 'Firmengelände' (company grounds) are common compounds.

details.etymology: From 'genesen' (to recover/convalesce), from Old High German 'ginesan' (to be saved/recover), related to Gothic 'ganisan'.
examples: Ich wünsche Ihnen eine schnelle Genesung. (I wish you a speedy recovery.)
Die Genesung nach der Operation dauerte lange. (The recovery after the surgery took a long time.)
details.usage: A somewhat formal word; 'Gute Besserung!' (Get well soon!) is the more common everyday phrase; 'Genesungswünsche' (wishes for recovery) is the standard compound.

details.etymology: From 'Gewerk' (trade/craft) and '-schaft' (suffix denoting a collective), originally meaning a guild or association of craftspeople.
examples: Die Gewerkschaft fordert höhere Löhne. (The union demands higher wages.)
Er ist Mitglied der Gewerkschaft. (He is a member of the union.)
details.usage: German unions are powerful institutions; 'Gewerkschaftsbund' (trade union federation) and 'gewerkschaftlich' (union-related) are important terms.

details.etymology: From Old High German 'gupf' (peak/top), possibly related to 'Kuppe' (hilltop/dome).
examples: Wir erreichten den Gipfel nach drei Stunden. (We reached the summit after three hours.)
Der G7-Gipfel findet in Deutschland statt. (The G7 summit takes place in Germany.)
details.usage: Means both a mountain 'summit' and a political 'summit meeting'; 'Gipfeltreffen' (summit meeting) and 'Berggipfel' (mountain peak) are standard compounds.

details.etymology: Compound of 'Grund' (ground/basis) and 'Satz' (sentence/principle), meaning a foundational statement or principle.
examples: Er handelt nach festen Grundsätzen. (He acts according to firm principles.)
Der Grundsatz der Gleichheit ist wichtig. (The principle of equality is important.)
details.usage: Used in legal, ethical, and organizational contexts; 'grundsätzlich' (fundamentally/in principle) is an extremely common adverb in German.

details.etymology: Borrowed from Latin 'horizon', from Greek 'horizon kyklos' (bounding circle), from 'horizein' (to bound/limit).
examples: Die Sonne verschwand am Horizont. (The sun disappeared on the horizon.)
Reisen erweitert den Horizont. (Traveling broadens the horizon.)
details.usage: Often used figuratively for intellectual breadth; 'den Horizont erweitern' (to broaden one's horizons) is a very popular phrase.

details.etymology: Borrowed from French 'initiative', from Latin 'initiare' (to begin), from 'initium' (beginning).
examples: Er ergriff die Initiative. (He took the initiative.)
Die Bürgerinitiative sammelte Unterschriften. (The citizens' initiative collected signatures.)
details.usage: Used in politics, business, and personal contexts; 'Bürgerinitiative' (citizens' initiative) is an important form of civic engagement in Germany.

details.etymology: Borrowed from Latin 'instinctus' (impulse/instigation), from 'instinguere' (to impel/instigate).
examples: Sein Instinkt hat ihn gerettet. (His instinct saved him.)
Tiere handeln oft aus Instinkt. (Animals often act out of instinct.)
details.usage: Used for both animal instincts and human intuition; 'instinktiv' (instinctive) is the common adjective.

details.etymology: Borrowed from Latin 'capitale' (principal sum), from 'caput' (head), originally referring to the 'head' or principal amount of money.
examples: Für die Gründung braucht man Kapital. (You need capital to start a business.)
Bildung ist das beste Kapital. (Education is the best capital.)
details.usage: Fundamental in economics; 'Kapitalismus' (capitalism), 'Kapitalanlage' (capital investment), and 'Humankapital' (human capital) are essential terms.

details.etymology: Borrowed from Latin 'compromissum' (mutual promise), from 'compromittere' (to promise mutually).
examples: Wir müssen einen Kompromiss finden. (We must find a compromise.)
Er ist zu keinem Kompromiss bereit. (He is not willing to compromise.)
details.usage: Central to German political culture, especially coalition politics; 'kompromissbereit' (willing to compromise) and 'kompromisslos' (uncompromising) are key adjectives.

details.etymology: Borrowed from Latin 'conceptum' (something conceived), from 'concipere' (to take in/conceive).
examples: Wir brauchen ein neues Konzept. (We need a new concept.)
Das Konzept wurde vom Team erarbeitet. (The concept was developed by the team.)
details.usage: Used for plans, drafts, and conceptual frameworks; 'konzeptionell' (conceptual) is the adjective, and 'Sicherheitskonzept' (security concept) is a common compound.

details.etymology: Compound of 'Kreis' (circle) and 'Lauf' (run/course), meaning a circular course or cycle.
examples: Der Kreislauf des Wassers ist faszinierend. (The water cycle is fascinating.)
Er hat Probleme mit dem Kreislauf. (He has circulation problems.)
details.usage: Means both 'cycle' (natural, economic) and 'circulation' (blood/cardiovascular); 'Kreislaufprobleme' (circulation problems) and 'Wirtschaftskreislauf' (economic cycle) are standard.

details.etymology: From 'leiten' (to lead/conduct), from Old High German 'leitan', meaning the act of leading or a conduit.
examples: Die Leitung des Projekts hat Frau Müller übernommen. (Ms. Müller has taken over the management of the project.)
Die Wasserleitung ist undicht. (The water pipe is leaking.)
details.usage: Means both 'management/leadership' and 'pipe/cable/line'; 'Telefonleitung' (phone line), 'Stromleitung' (power line), and 'Projektleitung' (project management) are common.

details.etymology: Compound of 'merken' (to notice/remember) and 'Mal' (mark/sign), meaning a noticeable characteristic.
examples: Ein wichtiges Merkmal dieser Art ist die rote Farbe. (An important characteristic of this species is the red color.)
Welche Merkmale hat das gesuchte Fahrzeug? (What features does the wanted vehicle have?)
details.usage: Used in science, technology, and descriptions; 'Qualitätsmerkmal' (quality feature) and 'Unterscheidungsmerkmal' (distinguishing feature) are common compounds.

details.etymology: From 'mischen' (to mix), from Latin 'miscere' (to mix), meaning the result of mixing.
examples: Die Mischung aus Theorie und Praxis ist ideal. (The mix of theory and practice is ideal.)
Diese Teemischung schmeckt hervorragend. (This tea blend tastes excellent.)
details.usage: Used for physical mixtures and figurative combinations; 'Teemischung' (tea blend) and 'Farbmischung' (color mixture) are typical compounds.

details.etymology: Compound of 'ober' (upper) and 'Fläche' (surface/area), meaning the upper or outer surface.
examples: Die Oberfläche des Sees war spiegelglatt. (The surface of the lake was mirror-smooth.)
An der Oberfläche sieht alles gut aus. (On the surface, everything looks fine.)
details.usage: Used literally and figuratively; 'Benutzeroberfläche' (user interface) is a key IT term, and 'oberflächlich' (superficial) is the common adjective.

details.etymology: Borrowed from Latin 'perspectiva' (the science of optics), from 'perspicere' (to look through), combining 'per' (through) and 'specere' (to look).
examples: Aus meiner Perspektive ist das richtig. (From my perspective, that is correct.)
Die jungen Leute brauchen berufliche Perspektiven. (Young people need career prospects.)
details.usage: Means both 'perspective/viewpoint' and 'prospect/outlook'; 'Zukunftsperspektive' (future prospect) and 'Perspektivwechsel' (change of perspective) are common.

details.etymology: Borrowed from Latin 'productio' (a lengthening/production), from 'producere' (to bring forth).
examples: Die Produktion wurde erhöht. (Production was increased.)
Die Filmproduktion dauerte zwei Jahre. (The film production took two years.)
details.usage: Used in manufacturing, film, and music; 'Massenproduktion' (mass production) and 'Produktionskosten' (production costs) are key business terms.

details.etymology: Borrowed from Latin 'programma', from Greek 'programma' (public notice), combining 'pro' (before) and 'gramma' (writing).
examples: Was steht heute auf dem Programm? (What is on the program today?)
Dieses Computerprogramm ist sehr nützlich. (This computer program is very useful.)
details.usage: Used for schedules, TV channels, software, and political platforms; 'Wahlprogramm' (election manifesto) and 'Rahmenprogramm' (supporting program) are standard compounds.

details.etymology: Borrowed from French 'ressource' (resource/remedy), from Old French 'resourdre' (to rise again), from Latin 'resurgere'.
examples: Wir müssen unsere Ressourcen besser nutzen. (We must use our resources better.)
Natürliche Ressourcen sind begrenzt. (Natural resources are limited.)
details.usage: Pronounced with French stress on the last syllable; 'Ressourcenknappheit' (scarcity of resources) and 'ressourcenschonend' (resource-efficient) are important compounds.

details.etymology: From Old High German 'scado' (damage/harm), of uncertain further origin, possibly related to Greek 'askethe' (unharmed).
examples: Der Sturm hat großen Schaden angerichtet. (The storm caused great damage.)
Schaden melden Sie bitte der Versicherung. (Please report damage to the insurance company.)
details.usage: Central to insurance and law; 'Schadensersatz' (compensation for damages), 'Sachschaden' (property damage), and 'Personenschaden' (personal injury) are key legal terms.

details.etymology: From 'schließen' (to close/conclude), from Old High German 'sliozan', meaning the closing or conclusion.
examples: Zum Schluss möchte ich mich bedanken. (In conclusion, I would like to express my thanks.)
Er zog die richtigen Schlüsse. (He drew the right conclusions.)
details.usage: Means 'end', 'conclusion' (logical), and 'closing'; 'Schluss machen' (to break up/end it) and 'Schlussfolgerung' (conclusion/inference) are very common.

details.etymology: From Old High German 'sculd' (debt/guilt/obligation), related to 'sollen' (shall/should), meaning what one owes.
examples: Er hat hohe Schulden. (He has large debts.)
Die Schulden müssen zurückgezahlt werden. (The debts must be repaid.)
details.usage: Usually used in the plural for financial debts; related to 'Schuld' (guilt/fault), reflecting the German cultural link between debt and moral obligation; 'Schuldner' (debtor) is the standard derivative.

details.etymology: Compound of 'schwer' (heavy) and 'Punkt' (point), originally a physics term meaning 'center of gravity', later extended to 'focus/emphasis'.
examples: Der Schwerpunkt des Kurses liegt auf Grammatik. (The focus of the course is on grammar.)
Wir müssen neue Schwerpunkte setzen. (We need to set new priorities.)
details.usage: Used in physics (center of gravity) and figuratively for 'main focus/priority'; 'Schwerpunktthema' (main topic/focus area) is a common compound.

details.etymology: Compound of 'Stand' (standing) and 'Punkt' (point), literally 'standing point,' calqued from Latin 'punctum standi' to mean a viewpoint or position.
examples: Ich verstehe deinen Standpunkt, aber ich bin anderer Meinung. (I understand your point of view, but I disagree.)
Von meinem Standpunkt aus ist das die beste Lösung. (From my point of view, that is the best solution.)
details.usage: Commonly used in debates and discussions, often in the phrase 'einen Standpunkt vertreten' (to represent a viewpoint).

details.etymology: From Middle High German 'strecke,' related to 'strecken' (to stretch), referring to a stretched-out distance or route.
examples: Die Strecke zwischen Berlin und München ist etwa 600 Kilometer lang. (The distance between Berlin and Munich is about 600 kilometers.)
Er ist die ganze Strecke zu Fuß gelaufen. (He walked the entire distance on foot.)
details.usage: Used for distances, routes, and railway lines; also appears in the idiom 'auf der Strecke bleiben' (to fall by the wayside).

details.etymology: Borrowed from Latin 'summa' (the highest, total), entering German through medieval scholarly and commercial language.
examples: Die Summe aller Kosten beträgt 500 Euro. (The total of all costs amounts to 500 euros.)
Er hat eine große Summe Geld gespendet. (He donated a large sum of money.)
details.usage: Frequently used in financial and mathematical contexts, and in the expression 'in der Summe' (all in all, on the whole).

details.etymology: Borrowed from Latin 'systema,' which came from Greek 'systema' (organized whole, composition), from 'synistanai' (to combine).
examples: Das Bildungssystem muss reformiert werden. (The education system needs to be reformed.)
Unser Computersystem wurde aktualisiert. (Our computer system was updated.)
details.usage: Extremely productive in compounds such as 'Gesundheitssystem' (health system), 'Sonnensystem' (solar system), and 'Betriebssystem' (operating system).

details.etymology: Borrowed from Greek 'therapeia' (healing, service), from 'therapeuein' (to treat, to serve).
examples: Der Arzt hat eine neue Therapie vorgeschlagen. (The doctor suggested a new therapy.)
Die Therapie hat ihr sehr geholfen. (The therapy helped her a lot.)
details.usage: Common in medical and psychological contexts, forming compounds like 'Physiotherapie,' 'Chemotherapie,' and 'Verhaltenstherapie' (behavioral therapy).

details.etymology: Compound of 'um' (around) and 'Frage' (question), literally meaning 'asking around,' coined in the 19th century for systematic polling.
examples: Laut einer aktuellen Umfrage sind 60 Prozent der Bürger zufrieden. (According to a recent survey, 60 percent of citizens are satisfied.)
Wir haben eine Umfrage unter den Mitarbeitern durchgeführt. (We conducted a survey among the employees.)
details.usage: Widely used in media and politics, often in combinations like 'Meinungsumfrage' (opinion poll) and 'Umfrageergebnis' (survey result).

details.etymology: Derived from the verb 'verändern' (to change), itself from 'ändern' (to alter) with the prefix 'ver-,' plus the nominalization suffix '-ung.'
examples: Die Veränderung des Klimas betrifft uns alle. (The change in climate affects us all.)
Er hat in seinem Leben viele Veränderungen erlebt. (He has experienced many changes in his life.)
details.usage: A very common noun for expressing change or transformation, used in everyday speech, politics, and science alike.

details.etymology: From the verb 'versammeln' (to gather, assemble), derived from 'sammeln' (to collect) with the prefix 'ver-,' plus '-ung.'
examples: Die Versammlung beginnt um 18 Uhr. (The assembly begins at 6 PM.)
Alle Mitglieder wurden zur Versammlung eingeladen. (All members were invited to the assembly.)
details.usage: Used in formal and political contexts; 'Versammlungsfreiheit' (freedom of assembly) is a constitutionally protected right in Germany.

details.etymology: Derived from 'verstehen' (to understand), via 'verständig' (sensible), with the suffix '-nis' forming an abstract noun.
examples: Ich habe volles Verständnis für deine Situation. (I have complete understanding for your situation.)
Wir bitten um Ihr Verständnis. (We ask for your understanding.)
details.usage: Extremely common in polite requests and apologies; the plural is rarely used, as it typically refers to an abstract concept.

details.etymology: From 'versorgen' (to supply, provide for), composed of 'ver-' and 'sorgen' (to care), plus the suffix '-ung.'
examples: Die medizinische Versorgung auf dem Land muss verbessert werden. (Medical care in rural areas needs to be improved.)
Die Versorgung mit Trinkwasser ist gesichert. (The supply of drinking water is secured.)
details.usage: Common in healthcare and infrastructure contexts, forming compounds like 'Stromversorgung' (power supply) and 'Grundversorgung' (basic provision).

details.etymology: From Middle High German 'verzicht,' derived from 'verzihen' (to renounce), related to 'ziehen' (to pull) with the prefix 'ver-' indicating removal.
examples: Der Verzicht auf Zucker fällt vielen Menschen schwer. (Giving up sugar is hard for many people.)
Er erklärte seinen Verzicht auf das Amt. (He declared his renunciation of the office.)
details.usage: Often used with 'auf' plus accusative to indicate what is being renounced; the plural is rare as it usually refers to a general concept.

details.etymology: Compound of 'vor' (before, forward) and 'Gang' (course, going), literally a 'going forward,' referring to a process or event.
examples: Der Vorgang wurde von der Polizei untersucht. (The incident was investigated by the police.)
Bitte beschreiben Sie den Vorgang genau. (Please describe the process precisely.)
details.usage: Used both for bureaucratic files/cases and for processes or events; in office language, it often means a case file or dossier.

details.etymology: From Middle High German 'vorrāt,' a compound of 'vor' (before) and 'Rat' (counsel, provision), originally meaning provisions set aside.
examples: Wir haben genug Vorräte für den Winter. (We have enough supplies for the winter.)
Der Vorrat an Öl geht langsam zur Neige. (The supply of oil is slowly running out.)
details.usage: Commonly used for stockpiles and supplies, appearing in compounds like 'Lebensmittelvorrat' (food supply) and the phrase 'auf Vorrat kaufen' (to stock up).

details.etymology: From Old High German 'wāfan,' related to Old English 'wæpen' (weapon), from a common Germanic root.
examples: Der Besitz von Waffen ist in Deutschland streng reguliert. (The possession of weapons is strictly regulated in Germany.)
Wissen ist die stärkste Waffe. (Knowledge is the most powerful weapon.)
details.usage: Used both literally and figuratively; forms many compounds like 'Schusswaffe' (firearm), 'Waffenstillstand' (ceasefire), and 'Waffenschein' (weapons permit).

details.etymology: From Old High German 'wala,' related to 'wählen' (to choose), from a Germanic root meaning 'to wish' or 'to choose.'
examples: Die nächste Wahl findet im September statt. (The next election takes place in September.)
Du hast die Wahl zwischen zwei Optionen. (You have the choice between two options.)
details.usage: Central in political discourse ('Bundestagswahl,' 'Landtagswahl') and everyday language for choices; 'die Qual der Wahl' means 'the agony of choice.'

details.etymology: Derived from 'zahlen' (to pay), from Old High German 'zalon,' ultimately related to 'Zahl' (number), plus the suffix '-ung.'
examples: Die Zahlung ist bis Ende des Monats fällig. (The payment is due by the end of the month.)
Wir akzeptieren verschiedene Zahlungen. (We accept various forms of payment.)
details.usage: Standard in financial and commercial contexts, forming compounds like 'Ratenzahlung' (installment payment) and 'Zahlungsmittel' (means of payment).

details.etymology: Compound of 'Zeit' (time) and 'Raum' (space, room), conceptualizing a span of time as a spatial metaphor.
examples: Der Zeitraum für die Bewerbung endet am Freitag. (The application period ends on Friday.)
Über einen Zeitraum von zehn Jahren hat sich viel verändert. (Over a period of ten years, a lot has changed.)
details.usage: Common in formal and administrative language to define periods; often used with 'über einen Zeitraum von' (over a period of).

details.etymology: From 'zunehmen' (to increase), composed of 'zu' (to) and 'nehmen' (to take), nominalized to describe the act of increasing.
examples: Die Zunahme der Bevölkerung stellt eine Herausforderung dar. (The increase in population poses a challenge.)
Es gab eine deutliche Zunahme der Temperaturen. (There was a clear increase in temperatures.)
details.usage: Used in statistics, science, and news reporting; the opposite is 'Abnahme' (decrease).

details.etymology: From 'anlegen' (to lay out, invest, create), composed of 'an' and 'legen,' plus nominalization; the word developed multiple meanings over time.
examples: Die Anlage wurde für fünf Millionen Euro gebaut. (The facility was built for five million euros.)
Er hat sein Geld in eine sichere Anlage investiert. (He invested his money in a safe investment.)
details.usage: A highly polysemous word meaning facility, investment, attachment (to a letter), or natural talent, depending on context.

details.etymology: From 'aufklären' (to clear up, enlighten), composed of 'auf' (up) and 'klären' (to clarify); historically became the German term for the Enlightenment era.
examples: Die Aufklärung des Verbrechens dauerte mehrere Monate. (Solving the crime took several months.)
Die Aufklärung war eine wichtige Epoche der europäischen Geschichte. (The Enlightenment was an important epoch of European history.)
details.usage: Refers to the Enlightenment era (capitalized), crime solving, sexual education ('sexuelle Aufklärung'), or military reconnaissance.

details.etymology: From 'auswirken' (to have an effect), composed of 'aus' (out) and 'wirken' (to work, have effect), plus '-ung.'
examples: Die Auswirkungen des Klimawandels sind weltweit spürbar. (The effects of climate change are felt worldwide.)
Diese Entscheidung hat positive Auswirkungen auf die Wirtschaft. (This decision has positive effects on the economy.)
details.usage: Very common in news and academic language, typically used in the plural with 'auf' to indicate what is affected.

details.etymology: From 'begeistern' (to inspire, enthuse), derived from 'Geist' (spirit) with the prefix 'be-,' literally meaning 'to fill with spirit.'
examples: Die Begeisterung der Fans war riesig. (The enthusiasm of the fans was enormous.)
Er erzählte mit großer Begeisterung von seiner Reise. (He talked about his trip with great enthusiasm.)
details.usage: The plural is rarely used; commonly appears in phrases like 'vor Begeisterung' (with enthusiasm) and 'Begeisterung wecken' (to spark enthusiasm).

details.etymology: From 'bemühen' (to make an effort), derived from 'Mühe' (effort, trouble) with the prefix 'be-,' plus '-ung.'
examples: Trotz aller Bemühungen konnte er das Problem nicht lösen. (Despite all efforts, he could not solve the problem.)
Wir danken Ihnen für Ihre Bemühungen. (We thank you for your efforts.)
details.usage: Most commonly used in the plural; a polite and somewhat formal word for efforts, frequently seen in business correspondence.

details.etymology: From 'binden' (to bind, tie), from Old High German 'bintan,' plus the suffix '-ung' to form the abstract noun.
examples: Die Bindung zwischen Mutter und Kind ist sehr stark. (The bond between mother and child is very strong.)
Er hat keine emotionale Bindung an diesen Ort. (He has no emotional attachment to this place.)
details.usage: Used for emotional bonds, ski bindings ('Skibindung'), and chemical bonds ('chemische Bindung'); also means commitment in relationships.

details.etymology: From 'einkommen' (to come in), composed of 'ein' (in) and 'kommen' (to come), referring to money that 'comes in.'
examples: Das durchschnittliche Einkommen in Deutschland liegt bei etwa 40.000 Euro im Jahr. (The average income in Germany is about 40,000 euros per year.)
Sein Einkommen reicht kaum zum Leben. (His income is barely enough to live on.)
details.usage: A formal term for income, used in compounds like 'Einkommensteuer' (income tax) and 'Grundeinkommen' (basic income).

details.etymology: From Old High German 'arnod,' related to 'Herbst' (autumn) and ultimately from a Proto-Germanic root meaning 'harvest time.'
examples: Die Ernte war dieses Jahr besonders gut. (The harvest was particularly good this year.)
Die Bauern beginnen im August mit der Ernte. (The farmers begin the harvest in August.)
details.usage: Used literally for agricultural harvest and figuratively in expressions like 'die Früchte seiner Arbeit ernten' (to reap the fruits of one's labor).

details.etymology: Compound of 'Fach' (subject, specialty) and 'Mann' (man), with the gender-neutral plural 'Fachleute' using 'Leute' (people).
examples: Sie sollten einen Fachmann zu Rate ziehen. (You should consult an expert.)
Er ist ein Fachmann auf dem Gebiet der Informatik. (He is an expert in the field of computer science.)
details.usage: The plural 'Fachleute' is gender-neutral; the feminine form is 'Fachfrau,' and 'Fachkraft' is an increasingly used gender-neutral singular alternative.

details.etymology: From 'feststellen' (to determine, establish), composed of 'fest' (firm) and 'stellen' (to place), plus '-ung.'
examples: Die Feststellung der Identität ist notwendig. (Establishing the identity is necessary.)
Er machte eine interessante Feststellung. (He made an interesting observation.)
details.usage: Common in legal, administrative, and academic language; can mean a finding, determination, observation, or statement of fact.

details.etymology: Compound of 'fort' (forward, onward) and 'Bildung' (education), meaning continuing education or further training.
examples: Die Firma bietet regelmäßige Fortbildungen an. (The company offers regular training courses.)
Ich nehme nächste Woche an einer Fortbildung teil. (I'm attending a training course next week.)
details.usage: Important in German professional culture where 'Fortbildung' is often employer-sponsored; distinct from 'Weiterbildung' which can be broader.

details.etymology: Compound of 'Grund' (foundation, ground) and 'Recht' (right, law), literally a 'foundational right.'
examples: Die Meinungsfreiheit ist ein Grundrecht. (Freedom of expression is a fundamental right.)
Die Grundrechte sind im Grundgesetz verankert. (Fundamental rights are enshrined in the Basic Law.)
details.usage: Central to German constitutional law; the first 19 articles of the Grundgesetz (Basic Law) define the Grundrechte.

details.etymology: From 'haften' (to be liable, to stick), from Old High German 'haften,' plus the suffix '-ung.'
examples: Die Haftung für Schäden liegt beim Hersteller. (Liability for damages lies with the manufacturer.)
Die Firma übernimmt keine Haftung für Verluste. (The company assumes no liability for losses.)
details.usage: A key legal term; 'GmbH' (Gesellschaft mit beschränkter Haftung) means 'company with limited liability,' one of Germany's most common business forms.

context: Der ___ des Abends war die Rede.

details.etymology: Compound of 'Höhe' (height) and 'Punkt' (point), literally 'high point,' denoting a peak or climax.
examples: Das Feuerwerk war der Höhepunkt des Abends. (The fireworks were the highlight of the evening.)
Die Krise hat ihren Höhepunkt erreicht. (The crisis has reached its peak.)
details.usage: Used for highlights of events, peaks of crises, and climaxes in narratives; a versatile word in both formal and informal contexts.

details.etymology: From Middle High German 'inhalt,' composed of 'in' (in) and 'Halt' (hold), referring to what is held within something.
examples: Der Inhalt des Buches ist sehr spannend. (The content of the book is very exciting.)
Bitte überprüfen Sie den Inhalt der Tasche. (Please check the contents of the bag.)
details.usage: Used for both physical contents and abstract content; 'Inhaltsverzeichnis' (table of contents) is a common compound.

details.etymology: Borrowed from Latin 'instantia' (urgency, presence), via French 'instance,' adopted into German legal and administrative language.
examples: Der Fall ging durch alle Instanzen. (The case went through all levels of jurisdiction.)
Das Gericht ist die letzte Instanz. (The court is the final authority.)
details.usage: Primarily a legal term referring to levels of jurisdiction; also used in computing for software instances and figuratively for authorities.

details.etymology: Borrowed from Latin 'concurrentia' (a running together), from 'concurrere' (to run together, compete).
examples: Die Konkurrenz auf dem Markt ist sehr groß. (The competition in the market is very intense.)
Wir müssen uns gegen die Konkurrenz behaupten. (We must hold our own against the competition.)
details.usage: Used for both the concept of competition and for competitors themselves; 'konkurrenzlos' means 'without competition, unrivaled.'

details.etymology: Borrowed from Latin 'consequentia' (a following, consequence), from 'consequi' (to follow after).
examples: Jede Handlung hat Konsequenzen. (Every action has consequences.)
Er zog die Konsequenzen und trat zurück. (He accepted the consequences and resigned.)
details.usage: Means both 'consequence' and 'consistency/determination'; 'Konsequenzen ziehen' is a set phrase meaning to draw conclusions and act accordingly.

details.etymology: Borrowed from French 'controle' (counter-register, verification), ultimately from medieval Latin 'contrarotulus' (counter-roll).
examples: Die Polizei führte eine Kontrolle durch. (The police carried out an inspection.)
Er hat die Kontrolle über das Fahrzeug verloren. (He lost control of the vehicle.)
details.usage: Means both 'control' and 'inspection/check'; 'Grenzkontrolle' (border control), 'Qualitätskontrolle' (quality control) are common compounds.

details.etymology: From Old High German 'legar' (a lying place, bed), related to 'liegen' (to lie); the English word 'lager' (beer) derives from 'Lagerbier' (stored beer).
examples: Die Waren werden im Lager aufbewahrt. (The goods are stored in the warehouse.)
Die Flüchtlinge lebten monatelang im Lager. (The refugees lived in the camp for months.)
details.usage: Highly polysemous: warehouse, camp, bearing (mechanical), or political faction; also the origin of English 'lager' beer (stored beer).

details.etymology: Borrowed from Latin 'logica,' from Greek 'logike (techne)' meaning '(art) of reasoning,' from 'logos' (word, reason).
examples: Dein Argument entbehrt jeder Logik. (Your argument lacks any logic.)
Die Logik hinter dieser Entscheidung ist klar. (The logic behind this decision is clear.)
details.usage: Used in philosophy, mathematics, and everyday speech; the plural is rare and refers to different systems of logic.

details.etymology: From 'minder' (lesser, fewer) plus the suffix '-heit,' meaning a lesser group; modeled after the concept of majority ('Mehrheit').
examples: Die Rechte der Minderheiten müssen geschützt werden. (The rights of minorities must be protected.)
Nur eine Minderheit stimmte dagegen. (Only a minority voted against it.)
details.usage: Important in political and social discourse; 'Minderheitenschutz' (minority protection) is a key principle in German law.

details.etymology: Compound of 'Mitte' (middle) and 'Punkt' (point), literally 'middle point,' referring to a center or focal point.
examples: Sie steht gern im Mittelpunkt. (She likes to be the center of attention.)
Berlin ist der Mittelpunkt des kulturellen Lebens. (Berlin is the center of cultural life.)
details.usage: Used both literally (geometric center) and figuratively (center of attention); 'im Mittelpunkt stehen' means to be in the spotlight.

details.etymology: Compound of 'nach' (after, for) and 'Frage' (question), meaning either a follow-up question or demand (economic).
examples: Die Nachfrage nach Elektroautos steigt. (The demand for electric cars is rising.)
Auf Nachfrage bestätigte er die Information. (When asked, he confirmed the information.)
details.usage: A key economics term ('Angebot und Nachfrage' = supply and demand) that also means 'inquiry' or 'follow-up question' in everyday use.

details.etymology: From 'neigen' (to incline, tilt), from Old High German 'neigen,' plus '-ung,' meaning an inclination both physical and figurative.
examples: Er hat eine Neigung zur Übertreibung. (He has a tendency to exaggerate.)
Die Neigung des Dachs beträgt 30 Grad. (The slope of the roof is 30 degrees.)
details.usage: Used for physical slopes/inclines and for personal tendencies, preferences, or dispositions.

details.etymology: From 'überzeugen' (to convince), composed of 'über' (over) and 'zeugen' (to testify, bear witness), plus '-ung.'
examples: Sie handelt aus tiefer Überzeugung. (She acts out of deep conviction.)
Seine politischen Überzeugungen sind bekannt. (His political convictions are well known.)
details.usage: Commonly used for firm beliefs and convictions; 'aus Überzeugung' means 'out of conviction' and 'Überzeugungskraft' means 'persuasive power.'

details.etymology: From 'vorwerfen' (to accuse, literally 'to throw before'), composed of 'vor' (before) and 'Wurf' (throw).
examples: Er wies alle Vorwürfe zurück. (He rejected all accusations.)
Mach dir keine Vorwürfe! (Don't blame yourself!)
details.usage: Common in legal and personal contexts; 'jemandem Vorwürfe machen' means to blame someone, and 'sich Vorwürfe machen' means to blame oneself.

details.etymology: From Old High German 'suht' (illness), originally related to 'siechen' (to be ill); the modern meaning shifted to 'addiction' over centuries.
examples: Die Sucht nach Alkohol zerstörte sein Leben. (The addiction to alcohol destroyed his life.)
Internetsucht ist ein wachsendes Problem. (Internet addiction is a growing problem.)
details.usage: Originally meant 'illness' (as in 'Gelbsucht' = jaundice); now primarily means addiction, forming compounds like 'Drogensucht' and 'Spielsucht' (gambling addiction).

details.etymology: From Old High German 'pflega' (care, custody), related to 'pflegen' (to care for, to tend), from a Germanic root meaning 'to be responsible for.'
examples: Die Pflege alter Menschen ist eine wichtige Aufgabe. (Caring for elderly people is an important task.)
Das Auto braucht regelmäßige Pflege. (The car needs regular maintenance.)
details.usage: Central in healthcare discourse; 'Pflegeheim' (nursing home), 'Pflegeversicherung' (care insurance), and 'Pflegekraft' (caregiver) are key compounds.

details.etymology: From 'absetzen' (to set down, sell off), composed of 'ab' (off) and 'Satz' (setting), with multiple meanings developing over time.
examples: Der Absatz des Produkts ist gestiegen. (Sales of the product have increased.)
Lesen Sie bitte den zweiten Absatz. (Please read the second paragraph.)
details.usage: A polysemous word meaning paragraph, heel (of a shoe), sales volume, or landing (on stairs), depending on context.

details.etymology: From 'auftragen' (to commission, apply), composed of 'auf' (on) and 'tragen' (to carry), originally meaning something laid upon someone.
examples: Die Firma hat einen großen Auftrag erhalten. (The company received a large order.)
Im Auftrag des Präsidenten wurde eine Untersuchung eingeleitet. (An investigation was initiated on behalf of the president.)
details.usage: Key in business and legal language; 'im Auftrag' (on behalf of) is abbreviated 'i.A.' in German correspondence.

details.etymology: Compound of 'an' (on, at) and 'Reiz' (stimulus, appeal), literally a stimulus directed toward something, meaning incentive.
examples: Steuervorteile schaffen einen Anreiz für Investitionen. (Tax advantages create an incentive for investments.)
Es fehlt der Anreiz, mehr zu arbeiten. (There is no incentive to work more.)
details.usage: Common in economics and policy discussions; 'Anreizsystem' (incentive system) and 'finanzieller Anreiz' (financial incentive) are frequent collocations.

details.etymology: From 'Bund' (federation, bond) plus the suffix '-nis,' related to 'binden' (to bind), denoting a formalized alliance.
examples: Die Länder schlossen ein Bündnis gegen den gemeinsamen Feind. (The countries formed an alliance against the common enemy.)
Das Bündnis für Arbeit setzt sich für Beschäftigung ein. (The alliance for jobs advocates for employment.)
details.usage: Used in political and military contexts; 'Bündnis 90/Die Grünen' is the full name of Germany's Green party.

details.etymology: From 'abwechseln' (to alternate, vary), composed of 'ab' (off) and 'wechseln' (to change), plus '-ung.'
examples: Zur Abwechslung könnten wir heute mal ins Kino gehen. (For a change, we could go to the cinema today.)
Der Job bietet viel Abwechslung. (The job offers a lot of variety.)
details.usage: Often used with 'zur Abwechslung' (for a change); 'abwechslungsreich' (varied, diverse) is a common derived adjective.

details.etymology: From 'achten' (to respect, pay attention to), from Old High German 'ahtōn,' plus '-ung'; also used as an interjection meaning 'attention!'
examples: Achtung! Der Zug fährt ein. (Attention! The train is arriving.)
Er hat große Achtung vor seinem Lehrer. (He has great respect for his teacher.)
details.usage: Functions both as an interjection (warning: 'Achtung!') and a noun meaning respect or esteem; the plural is virtually never used.

details.etymology: From 'anpassen' (to adapt, adjust), composed of 'an' (to) and 'passen' (to fit), plus '-ung.'
examples: Die Anpassung an das neue Klima dauerte Monate. (Adapting to the new climate took months.)
Es sind einige Anpassungen am Vertrag nötig. (Some adjustments to the contract are necessary.)
details.usage: Used in biology (adaptation), technology (adjustment), and everyday life; 'Anpassungsfähigkeit' means adaptability.

details.etymology: From 'aufregen' (to excite, upset), composed of 'auf' (up) and 'regen' (to stir), plus '-ung.'
examples: Die Aufregung vor der Prüfung war groß. (The excitement before the exam was great.)
Nur keine Aufregung! (Don't get upset!)
details.usage: Can mean both excitement and agitation/upset; 'sich aufregen' is a very common reflexive verb meaning to get worked up.

details.etymology: From 'auslösen' (to trigger, release), composed of 'aus' (out) and 'lösen' (to loosen, release), plus the agent suffix '-er.'
examples: Stress war der Auslöser für seine Krankheit. (Stress was the trigger for his illness.)
Drücken Sie den Auslöser der Kamera. (Press the shutter release of the camera.)
details.usage: Means both 'trigger/cause' in an abstract sense and 'shutter release' on a camera; the plural is identical to the singular.

details.etymology: From 'ausreden' (to talk one's way out, finish speaking), composed of 'aus' (out) and 'Rede' (speech), referring to speech used to get out of something.
examples: Das ist nur eine Ausrede! (That's just an excuse!)
Er hat immer eine Ausrede parat. (He always has an excuse ready.)
details.usage: Has a negative connotation implying a dishonest or unconvincing excuse, unlike 'Entschuldigung' which is a genuine apology or justification.

details.etymology: Compound of 'Bau' (building, construction) and 'Werk' (work, creation), referring to a constructed structure.
examples: Der Kölner Dom ist ein beeindruckendes Bauwerk. (Cologne Cathedral is an impressive structure.)
Dieses Bauwerk stammt aus dem 18. Jahrhundert. (This structure dates from the 18th century.)
details.usage: A formal term for buildings and structures, often used for architecturally or historically significant constructions.

details.etymology: From 'befinden' (to find, judge), composed of 'be-' and 'finden' (to find), referring to what has been found upon examination.
examples: Der ärztliche Befund war positiv. (The medical findings were positive.)
Die Befunde der Untersuchung liegen noch nicht vor. (The examination results are not yet available.)
details.usage: Primarily used in medical and scientific contexts for findings or results; 'ohne Befund' (without findings, normal) is a standard medical abbreviation.

details.etymology: From 'begaben' (to endow with talent), related to 'Gabe' (gift), with the prefix 'be-' and suffix '-ung.'
examples: Sie hat eine besondere Begabung für Musik. (She has a special talent for music.)
Seine mathematische Begabung wurde früh erkannt. (His mathematical talent was recognized early.)
details.usage: Used for natural talents and gifts; 'Hochbegabung' (giftedness) and 'begabt' (talented) are common related words.

details.etymology: From 'begegnen' (to encounter, meet), from Middle High German 'begegenen,' composed of 'be-' and 'gegen' (toward), plus '-ung.'
examples: Die Begegnung mit ihm hat mein Leben verändert. (The encounter with him changed my life.)
Es war eine zufällige Begegnung. (It was a chance encounter.)
details.usage: Used for meaningful meetings or encounters, often with a sense of significance; in sports, it can also mean a match or fixture.

details.etymology: From 'behaupten' (to claim, assert), from Middle High German 'behoubeten,' related to 'Haupt' (head), plus '-ung.'
examples: Diese Behauptung ist nicht bewiesen. (This claim is not proven.)
Er stellte die Behauptung auf, dass er unschuldig sei. (He made the claim that he was innocent.)
details.usage: Common in debates and legal contexts; 'eine Behauptung aufstellen' (to make a claim) is the standard collocation.

details.etymology: Compound of 'bei' (with, at) and 'Leid' (suffering, sorrow), literally 'sharing in suffering,' expressing sympathy.
examples: Mein herzliches Beileid. (My heartfelt condolences.)
Er sprach der Familie sein Beileid aus. (He expressed his condolences to the family.)
details.usage: Almost exclusively used in the context of death and mourning; 'Beileid aussprechen' (to express condolences) is the standard phrase, and the plural is extremely rare.

details.etymology: From 'beteiligen' (to involve, participate), related to 'Teil' (part), with prefix 'be-' and suffix '-ung.'
examples: Die Beteiligung an der Wahl war hoch. (Voter turnout was high.)
Das Unternehmen hält eine Beteiligung an der Firma. (The company holds a stake in the firm.)
details.usage: Means both participation (e.g., voter turnout) and financial stake/shareholding in business contexts.

details.etymology: From 'betrügen' (to deceive, cheat), from Middle High German 'betriegen,' composed of 'be-' and 'trügen' (to deceive).
examples: Er wurde wegen Betrugs verurteilt. (He was convicted of fraud.)
Das war glatter Betrug! (That was outright fraud!)
details.usage: A legal and everyday term; the plural 'Betrügereien' uses a different form and means 'fraudulent acts'; common in compounds like 'Steuerbetrug' (tax fraud).

details.etymology: From Middle High German 'botschaft,' derived from 'Bote' (messenger) plus '-schaft,' meaning a message brought by a messenger.
examples: Die deutsche Botschaft in Paris wurde renoviert. (The German embassy in Paris was renovated.)
Seine Rede enthielt eine wichtige Botschaft. (His speech contained an important message.)
details.usage: Has two main meanings: embassy (diplomatic) and message; 'Botschafter' (ambassador) is the derived agent noun.

details.etymology: From 'dankbar' (grateful), derived from 'Dank' (thanks) plus '-bar' (able) and the abstract suffix '-keit.'
examples: Sie empfand tiefe Dankbarkeit. (She felt deep gratitude.)
Ich möchte meine Dankbarkeit ausdrücken. (I would like to express my gratitude.)
details.usage: An abstract noun almost exclusively used in the singular; commonly paired with verbs like 'empfinden' (to feel) and 'ausdrücken' (to express).

details.etymology: From 'drohen' (to threaten), from Old High German 'drouwen,' plus the suffix '-ung.'
examples: Die Drohung wurde ernst genommen. (The threat was taken seriously.)
Er sprach eine Drohung gegen seinen Nachbarn aus. (He made a threat against his neighbor.)
details.usage: Common in legal and everyday contexts; 'leere Drohung' (empty threat) and 'Morddrohung' (death threat) are frequent collocations.

details.etymology: From 'eingreifen' (to intervene, reach in), composed of 'ein' (in) and 'greifen' (to grasp), referring to an act of reaching into something.
examples: Der chirurgische Eingriff verlief erfolgreich. (The surgical procedure went successfully.)
Das ist ein schwerer Eingriff in die Privatsphäre. (That is a serious intrusion into privacy.)
details.usage: Used for medical procedures (surgery) and for interference/intervention in rights or systems; both meanings are equally common.

context: Die ___ gilt bis Ende des Monats.

details.etymology: From 'einschränken' (to restrict, limit), composed of 'ein' (in) and 'Schranke' (barrier), plus '-ung.'
examples: Es gibt einige Einschränkungen bei der Nutzung. (There are some restrictions on usage.)
Die Einschränkung der Freiheit wurde kritisiert. (The restriction of freedom was criticized.)
details.usage: Very common in legal, medical, and everyday contexts for limitations and restrictions; often used in the plural.

details.etymology: Borrowed from Latin 'emissio' (a sending out), from 'emittere' (to send out); adopted into German for both environmental and financial contexts.
examples: Die CO2-Emissionen müssen reduziert werden. (CO2 emissions must be reduced.)
Die Emission neuer Aktien wurde genehmigt. (The issuance of new shares was approved.)
details.usage: Primarily used in environmental contexts for pollutant emissions, but also in finance for the issuance of securities.

details.etymology: Borrowed from French 'engagement' (commitment, involvement), from 'engager' (to pledge); retains French pronunciation in German.
examples: Ihr soziales Engagement ist bewundernswert. (Her social commitment is admirable.)
Der Schauspieler erhielt ein Engagement am Stadttheater. (The actor received an engagement at the city theater.)
details.usage: Means civic or social commitment in general use, or a professional engagement for performers; pronounced in the French manner in German.

details.etymology: From 'erlassen' (to issue, decree, waive), composed of 'er-' and 'lassen' (to let), meaning something officially issued or remitted.
examples: Der Erlass des Ministeriums trat sofort in Kraft. (The ministry's decree took effect immediately.)
Der Erlass der Schulden war eine große Erleichterung. (The remission of debts was a great relief.)
details.usage: Has two meanings: an official decree/edict, and the remission or waiving of debts or penalties.

details.etymology: From 'ertragen' in an older sense of 'to yield, produce,' composed of 'er-' and 'tragen' (to carry, bear), referring to what land or investment bears.
examples: Der Ertrag der Ernte war hoch. (The yield of the harvest was high.)
Die Erträge aus den Investitionen sind gestiegen. (The returns on investments have increased.)
details.usage: Used in agriculture for crop yield and in finance for returns or revenue; 'ertragreich' means profitable or high-yielding.

details.etymology: From 'Feind' (enemy), from Old High German 'fiant' (hating one), plus the abstract suffix '-schaft.'
examples: Zwischen den beiden Familien herrschte seit Jahrzehnten Feindschaft. (Enmity had existed between the two families for decades.)
Feindschaft führt nur zu Leid. (Enmity only leads to suffering.)
details.usage: The opposite of 'Freundschaft' (friendship); used in literature, history, and everyday speech to describe deep-seated hostility.

details.etymology: Compound of 'fest' (firm) and 'Nahme' (from 'nehmen,' to take), literally 'firm taking,' meaning arrest.
examples: Die Polizei meldete mehrere Festnahmen. (The police reported several arrests.)
Bei der Festnahme leistete der Verdächtige Widerstand. (The suspect resisted during the arrest.)
details.usage: A formal/official term for arrest, commonly used in news reporting; 'vorläufige Festnahme' (preliminary arrest) is a legal term.

details.etymology: From 'flüchten' (to flee), from Middle High German 'vlühten,' plus the suffix '-ling' denoting a person characterized by the action.
examples: Millionen von Flüchtlingen suchen Schutz in Europa. (Millions of refugees seek protection in Europe.)
Der Flüchtling erzählte seine Geschichte. (The refugee told his story.)
details.usage: A central term in German migration discourse since 2015; 'Flüchtlingskrise' (refugee crisis) and 'Flüchtlingslager' (refugee camp) are common compounds.

details.etymology: From 'gebrauchen' (to use), composed of 'ge-' and 'brauchen' (to need, use), from Old High German 'bruhhan.'
examples: Lesen Sie vor Gebrauch die Packungsbeilage. (Read the package insert before use.)
Das Wort ist nicht mehr im Gebrauch. (The word is no longer in use.)
details.usage: Means 'use/usage' in singular; the plural 'Gebräuche' means 'customs/traditions' — note the meaning shift between singular and plural.

details.etymology: From Middle High German 'gevare' (danger, ambush), related to 'fahren' (to travel), originally referring to perils encountered on a journey.
examples: Bei Gefahr drücken Sie den Notknopf. (In case of danger, press the emergency button.)
Die Gefahren des Rauchens sind bekannt. (The dangers of smoking are well known.)
details.usage: A fundamental vocabulary word; 'gefährlich' (dangerous) is the adjective, and compounds include 'Lebensgefahr' (mortal danger) and 'Gefahrenzone' (danger zone).

details.etymology: From 'gelassen' (calm, composed), past participle of 'lassen' (to let), plus '-heit'; the concept of 'letting go' became 'calmness.'
examples: Er reagierte mit bewundernswerter Gelassenheit. (He reacted with admirable composure.)
Gelassenheit ist in stressigen Zeiten wichtig. (Composure is important in stressful times.)
details.usage: A culturally valued concept in German-speaking countries; related to Meister Eckhart's mystical concept of 'Gelassenheit' as spiritual letting-go, later adopted by Heidegger.

details.etymology: From 'genießen' (to enjoy), from Old High German 'giniozan,' related to the concept of enjoyment and consumption.
examples: Das Essen war ein wahrer Genuss. (The meal was a true delight.)
Er trank den Wein mit Genuss. (He drank the wine with relish.)
details.usage: Used for pleasurable experiences, especially food and drink; 'Genussmittel' (luxury foods like coffee, alcohol, tobacco) is a common compound.

details.etymology: From Middle High German 'gerühte' (rumor, outcry), related to 'rufen' (to call) and 'Ruf' (reputation, call).
examples: Es kursieren Gerüchte über seinen Rücktritt. (Rumors about his resignation are circulating.)
Das Gerücht hat sich als falsch herausgestellt. (The rumor turned out to be false.)
details.usage: Commonly used with verbs like 'kursieren' (to circulate), 'verbreiten' (to spread), and 'streuen' (to scatter); 'Gerüchteküche' (rumor mill) is a vivid compound.

details.etymology: Compound of 'gleich' (equal) and 'Gewicht' (weight), literally 'equal weight,' meaning balance or equilibrium.
examples: Er verlor das Gleichgewicht und fiel hin. (He lost his balance and fell down.)
Das ökologische Gleichgewicht ist gestört. (The ecological balance is disturbed.)
details.usage: Used for physical balance, ecological equilibrium, and emotional stability; 'aus dem Gleichgewicht bringen' means to throw off balance.

details.etymology: From 'großzügig' (generous), composed of 'groß' (great, large) and 'Zug' (trait, pull) plus '-igkeit,' meaning having a 'big manner.'
examples: Seine Großzügigkeit war bekannt. (His generosity was well known.)
Wir danken Ihnen für Ihre Großzügigkeit. (We thank you for your generosity.)
details.usage: Refers to generosity both in material giving and in being broad-minded or tolerant; the plural is rarely used.

details.etymology: Compound of 'hinter' (behind) and 'Grund' (ground, reason), literally 'background,' referring to what lies behind something.
examples: Die Hintergründe des Konflikts sind komplex. (The background of the conflict is complex.)
Im Hintergrund spielte leise Musik. (Soft music was playing in the background.)
details.usage: Used both literally (visual background) and figuratively (background information); 'Hintergrundwissen' (background knowledge) is a useful compound.

details.etymology: From 'irren' (to err, wander), from Old High German 'irrōn,' plus the suffix '-tum' forming a noun denoting a state.
examples: Das war ein großer Irrtum. (That was a big mistake.)
Irrtümer vorbehalten. (Errors excepted / Subject to change.)
details.usage: A formal word for error or mistake; 'Irrtümer vorbehalten' is a standard legal/commercial disclaimer, and 'im Irrtum sein' means to be mistaken.

details.etymology: From Latin 'camera' (vaulted room), via Old High German 'chamara,' originally referring to a small private room.
examples: Die Ärztekammer hat neue Richtlinien veröffentlicht. (The medical chamber has published new guidelines.)
Das Herz hat vier Kammern. (The heart has four chambers.)
details.usage: Used for professional associations ('Handelskammer' = chamber of commerce), parliamentary chambers, anatomical chambers, and small rooms.

details.etymology: From 'klar' (clear), borrowed from Latin 'clarus' (bright, clear), plus the abstract suffix '-heit.'
examples: Wir brauchen Klarheit in dieser Angelegenheit. (We need clarity in this matter.)
Er verschaffte sich Klarheit über die Lage. (He gained clarity about the situation.)
details.usage: Commonly used in the phrase 'sich Klarheit verschaffen' (to gain clarity); the plural is extremely rare.

details.etymology: Borrowed from Latin 'colonia' (settlement, farm), from 'colonus' (farmer, settler), from 'colere' (to cultivate).
examples: Deutschland hatte bis 1919 mehrere Kolonien in Afrika. (Germany had several colonies in Africa until 1919.)
Die Ameisenkolonie umfasst tausende Tiere. (The ant colony comprises thousands of animals.)
details.usage: Used historically for colonial possessions and in biology for groups of organisms; 'Schrebergartenkolonie' refers to allotment garden communities in Germany.

details.etymology: Borrowed from Latin 'coniunctura' (combination of circumstances), from 'coniungere' (to join together), adopted into German economic vocabulary.
examples: Die Konjunktur erholt sich langsam. (The economy is slowly recovering.)
In Zeiten schlechter Konjunktur steigt die Arbeitslosigkeit. (In times of economic downturn, unemployment rises.)
details.usage: A key economics term for the business cycle; compounds include 'Konjunkturpaket' (stimulus package) and 'Konjunkturflaute' (economic slump).

details.etymology: Compound of 'Leben' (life) and 'Raum' (space, room), literally 'living space,' originally a neutral ecological term later politically tainted by Nazi ideology.
examples: Der Lebensraum vieler Tierarten ist bedroht. (The habitat of many animal species is threatened.)
Wir müssen natürliche Lebensräume schützen. (We must protect natural habitats.)
details.usage: Today primarily used in ecology for habitats; historically loaded due to Nazi expansionist ideology, so context matters when using this word.

details.etymology: Compound of 'leiten' (to lead, guide) and 'Stelle' (place, position), meaning a place from which operations are directed.
examples: Die Leitstelle koordiniert alle Rettungseinsätze. (The control center coordinates all rescue operations.)
Rufen Sie die Leitstelle an, wenn Sie Hilfe brauchen. (Call the control center if you need help.)
details.usage: Used for emergency dispatch centers, traffic control centers, and operational command posts; a key term in emergency services.

details.etymology: From 'mahnen' (to remind, admonish, urge payment), from Old High German 'manōn,' plus '-ung.'
examples: Er erhielt eine Mahnung wegen der unbezahlten Rechnung. (He received a reminder about the unpaid bill.)
Ihre Worte waren eine Mahnung an alle. (Her words were a warning to everyone.)
details.usage: Has two main uses: a payment reminder/dunning letter in business, and a warning or admonition in general; 'Mahngebühr' (reminder fee) is a common compound.

details.etymology: From 'Mensch' (human being), from Old High German 'mennisco,' plus the collective suffix '-heit.'
examples: Der Klimawandel betrifft die gesamte Menschheit. (Climate change affects all of humanity.)
Das ist ein großer Fortschritt für die Menschheit. (That is a great advance for humanity.)
details.usage: A collective noun referring to all of humankind; the plural is essentially never used since it already denotes the entirety of humanity.

details.etymology: From 'miss-' (wrong, bad) and 'Trauen' (trust), from 'trauen' (to trust), literally 'wrong trust' meaning distrust.
examples: Das Misstrauen gegenüber Politikern wächst. (Distrust toward politicians is growing.)
Sie betrachtete ihn mit Misstrauen. (She regarded him with suspicion.)
details.usage: An uncountable noun with no real plural; 'Misstrauensvotum' (vote of no confidence) is an important political compound in parliamentary systems.

details.etymology: Compound of 'sparen' (to save) and 'Maßnahme' (measure), referring to cost-cutting actions.
examples: Die Regierung hat strenge Sparmaßnahmen eingeführt. (The government introduced strict austerity measures.)
Die Sparmaßnahmen betreffen vor allem den Bildungsbereich. (The austerity measures primarily affect the education sector.)
details.usage: Frequently used in economic and political discourse, especially regarding government budgets; often used in the plural for austerity programs.

details.etymology: From 'niederlegen' (to lay down, as in arms), composed of 'nieder' (down) and 'Lage' (position), referring to being laid low in defeat.
examples: Die Mannschaft erlitt eine schwere Niederlage. (The team suffered a heavy defeat.)
Aus jeder Niederlage kann man lernen. (You can learn from every defeat.)
details.usage: Used in sports, politics, military, and personal contexts; the opposite is 'Sieg' (victory).

details.etymology: Borrowed from Latin 'paragraphus,' from Greek 'paragraphos' (a line drawn to mark a break), from 'para' (beside) and 'graphein' (to write).
examples: Laut Paragraph 218 des Strafgesetzbuches ist das verboten. (According to Section 218 of the Criminal Code, that is prohibited.)
Der Vertrag umfasst zwanzig Paragraphen. (The contract comprises twenty sections.)
details.usage: In German legal language, refers to sections of laws (marked with the symbol §); distinct from 'Absatz' which means paragraph in a text.

details.etymology: Borrowed from Latin 'provisio' (foresight, provision), via French; in German it narrowed to mean commission or brokerage fee.
examples: Der Makler erhält eine Provision von drei Prozent. (The broker receives a commission of three percent.)
Die Provisionen der Verkäufer wurden erhöht. (The salespeople's commissions were increased.)
details.usage: In German, 'Provision' specifically means a sales commission or brokerage fee, unlike English 'provision' which has broader meanings.

details.etymology: Compound of 'Rechnung' (account, bill) and 'Hof' (court), literally 'court of accounts,' an institution auditing public finances.
examples: Der Bundesrechnungshof prüft die Ausgaben der Regierung. (The Federal Court of Auditors examines government spending.)
Der Rechnungshof stellte Unregelmäßigkeiten fest. (The court of auditors found irregularities.)
details.usage: Refers to government audit institutions; the 'Bundesrechnungshof' is Germany's supreme audit institution, equivalent to a national audit office.

details.etymology: Compound of 'Rede' (speech) and 'Wendung' (turn, phrase), literally a 'turn of speech,' meaning an idiom or set expression.
examples: Das ist eine bekannte Redewendung. (That is a well-known idiom.)
Die deutsche Sprache hat viele interessante Redewendungen. (The German language has many interesting idioms.)
details.usage: The standard German term for 'idiom' or 'set phrase'; often used in language learning contexts.

details.etymology: From 'schöpfen' (to create, scoop), from Old High German 'skepfen,' plus '-ung'; related to the act of drawing something into being.
examples: Die Schöpfung der Welt wird in vielen Religionen beschrieben. (The creation of the world is described in many religions.)
Dieses Gemälde ist eine wahre Schöpfung. (This painting is a true creation.)
details.usage: Used in religious contexts for divine creation ('die Schöpfung' = Creation) and in artistic contexts for creative works; 'Schöpfer' means creator.

details.etymology: Compound of 'sehnen' (to long for) and 'Sucht' (in its old meaning of 'sickness'), literally a 'longing sickness,' a deeply German Romantic concept.
examples: Er hatte Sehnsucht nach seiner Heimat. (He longed for his homeland.)
Die Sehnsucht nach Freiheit trieb ihn an. (The longing for freedom drove him on.)
details.usage: A quintessentially German word often cited as untranslatable; it combines deep longing with melancholy and is central to German Romantic literature and music.

details.etymology: From 'sparsam' (thrifty, economical), derived from 'sparen' (to save) plus '-sam' and the abstract suffix '-keit.'
examples: Sparsamkeit ist eine wichtige Tugend. (Thriftiness is an important virtue.)
Durch Sparsamkeit konnte sie viel Geld zurücklegen. (Through frugality, she was able to save a lot of money.)
details.usage: Traditionally considered a German virtue ('schwäbische Sparsamkeit' = Swabian thriftiness is a stereotype); the plural is extremely rare.

details.etymology: Compound of 'Spiel' (play, game) and 'Raum' (room, space), literally 'play space,' meaning room to maneuver.
examples: Wir haben wenig Spielraum bei den Verhandlungen. (We have little room for maneuver in the negotiations.)
Das Budget lässt keinen Spielraum für zusätzliche Ausgaben. (The budget allows no room for additional spending.)
details.usage: Commonly used in business, politics, and everyday speech; also has a technical meaning in engineering for mechanical clearance or tolerance.

details.etymology: Compound of 'Staat' (state) and 'Anwalt' (lawyer, advocate), literally 'state lawyer,' meaning public prosecutor.
examples: Der Staatsanwalt forderte eine Freiheitsstrafe von fünf Jahren. (The prosecutor demanded a prison sentence of five years.)
Die Staatsanwälte ermitteln in dem Fall. (The prosecutors are investigating the case.)
details.usage: The feminine form is 'Staatsanwältin'; the office is 'Staatsanwaltschaft' (prosecution office/district attorney's office).

details.etymology: Compound of 'Stelle' (place, position) and 'Wert' (value), originally a mathematical term (place value) that extended to mean importance or significance.
examples: Bildung hat in unserer Gesellschaft einen hohen Stellenwert. (Education has a high status in our society.)
Der Stellenwert der Gesundheit wird oft unterschätzt. (The importance of health is often underestimated.)
details.usage: Commonly used with 'hoch' (high) or 'gering' (low) to describe the importance or priority of something in society or personal life.

details.etymology: From 'ausfallen' (to fall out, fail, be canceled), composed of 'aus' (out) and 'Fall' (fall), referring to something that drops out.
examples: Wegen des Stromausfalls mussten wir im Dunkeln sitzen. (Because of the power outage, we had to sit in the dark.)
Der Ausfall des Lehrers führte zum Unterrichtsausfall. (The teacher's absence led to class cancellation.)
details.usage: Used for failures, outages, cancellations, and absences; forms many compounds like 'Stromausfall' (power outage) and 'Haarausfall' (hair loss).

details.etymology: Borrowed from Latin 'tragoedia,' from Greek 'tragoidia' (goat song), composed of 'tragos' (goat) and 'oide' (song), originally a dramatic genre.
examples: Das Erdbeben war eine Tragödie für die Region. (The earthquake was a tragedy for the region.)
