Shakespeare schrieb viele berühmte Tragödien. (Shakespeare wrote many famous tragedies.)
details.usage: Used both for the dramatic genre and figuratively for disastrous events; 'tragisch' (tragic) is the derived adjective.

details.etymology: From Old High German 'trūra' (sorrow, grief), related to a Germanic root for drooping or being downcast.
examples: Die Familie ist in tiefer Trauer. (The family is in deep mourning.)
Er konnte seine Trauer nicht verbergen. (He could not hide his grief.)
details.usage: Used for grief and mourning; 'Trauerfeier' (funeral service), 'Trauertag' (day of mourning), and 'trauern' (to mourn) are common related words.

details.etymology: From 'übergehen' (to go over, transition), composed of 'über' (over) and 'Gang' (going, passage).
examples: Der Übergang von der Schule zum Beruf ist nicht einfach. (The transition from school to work is not easy.)
Benutzen Sie den Fußgängerübergang. (Use the pedestrian crossing.)
details.usage: Used for physical crossings ('Grenzübergang' = border crossing) and abstract transitions; 'Übergangsphase' (transitional phase) is a common compound.

details.etymology: From 'überlegen' (to consider, reflect), composed of 'über' (over) and 'legen' (to lay), literally 'to lay over in one's mind,' plus '-ung.'
examples: Nach reiflicher Überlegung habe ich mich entschieden. (After careful consideration, I have decided.)
Seine Überlegungen waren sehr klug. (His reflections were very wise.)
details.usage: Common in formal discourse; 'nach reiflicher Überlegung' (after careful/mature consideration) is a standard phrase.

details.etymology: From 'übertreiben' (to exaggerate), composed of 'über' (over, beyond) and 'treiben' (to drive), literally 'to drive beyond,' plus '-ung.'
examples: Das ist eine maßlose Übertreibung! (That is a gross exaggeration!)
Ohne Übertreibung kann man sagen, dass es ein Erfolg war. (Without exaggeration, one can say it was a success.)
details.usage: Common in everyday speech; 'ohne Übertreibung' (without exaggeration) is a frequent qualifier to emphasize truthfulness.

details.etymology: From 'umstellen' (to switch over, rearrange), composed of 'um' (around, over) and 'stellen' (to place), plus '-ung.'
examples: Die Umstellung auf erneuerbare Energien dauert Jahre. (The switch to renewable energies takes years.)
Die Umstellung der Uhren auf Sommerzeit erfolgt im März. (The clocks are changed to summer time in March.)
details.usage: Used for transitions, conversions, and adjustments in systems, diet, technology, and daily routines.

details.etymology: From 'verfassen' (to compose, write), composed of 'ver-' and 'fassen' (to grasp, compose), plus '-ung'; originally meaning 'composition' then extended to 'constitution.'
examples: Die deutsche Verfassung heißt Grundgesetz. (The German constitution is called the Basic Law.)
Er war in schlechter körperlicher Verfassung. (He was in poor physical condition.)
details.usage: Means both 'constitution' (legal document) and 'condition/state' (physical or mental); Germany's constitution is called 'Grundgesetz' rather than 'Verfassung' for historical reasons.

details.etymology: From 'vergüten' (to compensate, reimburse), related to 'gut' (good), with 'ver-' meaning 'to make good,' plus '-ung.'
examples: Die Vergütung für diese Stelle ist angemessen. (The compensation for this position is appropriate.)
Er erhielt eine Vergütung für seine Dienste. (He received remuneration for his services.)
details.usage: A formal term for compensation, remuneration, or reimbursement; commonly used in employment contracts and legal documents.

details.etymology: From 'vermitteln' (to mediate, arrange), related to 'Mitte' (middle), with 'ver-' meaning 'to place in the middle,' plus '-ung.'
examples: Die Vermittlung zwischen den Parteien war erfolgreich. (The mediation between the parties was successful.)
Die Arbeitsvermittlung half ihm, einen Job zu finden. (The employment agency helped him find a job.)
details.usage: Used for mediation, job placement, and the conveying of knowledge; 'Arbeitsvermittlung' (employment agency) and 'Wissensvermittlung' (knowledge transfer) are key compounds.

details.etymology: From Old High German 'firnumft,' related to 'vernehmen' (to perceive, hear), literally the capacity to perceive and understand rationally.
examples: Hör endlich auf die Vernunft! (Finally listen to reason!)
Die Vernunft hat in dieser Diskussion gesiegt. (Reason prevailed in this discussion.)
details.usage: A central concept in German philosophy, especially Kant's distinction between 'Vernunft' (reason) and 'Verstand' (understanding); the plural is extremely rare.

details.etymology: From 'verschmutzen' (to pollute, dirty), composed of 'ver-' and 'Schmutz' (dirt), plus '-ung.'
examples: Die Verschmutzung der Meere ist ein großes Problem. (Ocean pollution is a major problem.)
Die Verschmutzung der Luft hat zugenommen. (Air pollution has increased.)
details.usage: A key environmental term; 'Umweltverschmutzung' (environmental pollution), 'Luftverschmutzung' (air pollution), and 'Wasserverschmutzung' (water pollution) are standard compounds.

details.etymology: Compound of 'vor' (before, ahead) and 'Recht' (right), literally a 'prior right' or privilege.
examples: Bildung sollte kein Vorrecht der Reichen sein. (Education should not be a privilege of the wealthy.)
Er genoss besondere Vorrechte. (He enjoyed special privileges.)
details.usage: Used in legal and social contexts for privileges and prerogatives; synonymous with 'Privileg' but more Germanic in tone.

details.etymology: From Middle High German 'vorwant,' composed of 'vor' (before) and 'Wand' (wall), originally a screen or partition placed in front, figuratively a pretense.
examples: Unter dem Vorwand einer Krankheit blieb er zu Hause. (Under the pretext of an illness, he stayed home.)
Das war nur ein Vorwand, um nicht kommen zu müssen. (That was just a pretext to not have to come.)
details.usage: Always implies dishonesty or deception; commonly used with 'unter dem Vorwand' (under the pretext of).

details.etymology: Compound of 'Wahl' (election) and 'Kampf' (fight, battle), literally 'election fight,' meaning an election campaign.
examples: Der Wahlkampf wird immer intensiver. (The election campaign is getting increasingly intense.)
Im Wahlkampf machen Politiker viele Versprechen. (During election campaigns, politicians make many promises.)
details.usage: A standard political term; 'Wahlkampagne' is a synonym, and 'Wahlkampfveranstaltung' (campaign event) is a common compound.

details.etymology: From 'weise' (wise), from Old High German 'wīs,' plus the abstract suffix '-heit.'
examples: Mit dem Alter kommt die Weisheit. (With age comes wisdom.)
Das ist eine alte Weisheit. (That is an old piece of wisdom.)
details.usage: In the singular means wisdom as a quality; in the plural 'Weisheiten' means wise sayings or pieces of wisdom; 'Weisheitszahn' (wisdom tooth) is a common compound.

details.etymology: Compound of 'Wette' (bet, competition) and 'Lauf' (run, course), literally a 'competitive run' or race.
examples: Der Wettlauf ins All begann in den 1950er Jahren. (The space race began in the 1950s.)
Es ist ein Wettlauf gegen die Zeit. (It is a race against time.)
details.usage: Often used figuratively; 'Wettlauf gegen die Zeit' (race against time) and 'Wettrüsten' (arms race) are well-known expressions.

details.etymology: From Middle High German 'wilkür,' composed of 'Wille' (will) and 'Kür' (choice), originally meaning 'free choice' but shifting to mean 'arbitrariness.'
examples: Die Bürger protestierten gegen die Willkür der Regierung. (The citizens protested against the government's arbitrariness.)
Das Urteil war reine Willkür. (The verdict was pure arbitrariness.)
details.usage: Now exclusively negative, meaning arbitrary or despotic action; 'willkürlich' (arbitrary) is the derived adjective, and the plural is rare.

details.etymology: From Old High German 'wirdī,' related to 'wert' (worthy, valuable); Article 1 of Germany's Basic Law famously states 'Die Würde des Menschen ist unantastbar.'
examples: Die Würde des Menschen ist unantastbar. (Human dignity is inviolable.)
Er trug sein Schicksal mit Würde. (He bore his fate with dignity.)
details.usage: One of the most important words in German constitutional law; also means 'office' or 'rank' in formal contexts ('die Würde eines Amtes').

details.etymology: Compound of 'Zeit' (time) and 'Geist' (spirit, mind), coined by the philosopher Herder and popularized by Hegel to describe the spirit of an era.
examples: Dieses Buch spiegelt den Zeitgeist der 1920er Jahre wider. (This book reflects the spirit of the 1920s.)
Man muss den Zeitgeist verstehen, um die Mode zu begreifen. (One must understand the zeitgeist to comprehend fashion.)
details.usage: A German loanword used internationally in English and other languages; often used to describe cultural or intellectual trends of a particular era.

details.etymology: Borrowed from Latin 'censura' (assessment, censorship), from 'censor' (the Roman magistrate who assessed citizens and morals).
examples: Die Zensur der Presse wurde abgeschafft. (Press censorship was abolished.)
Er bekam gute Zensuren in der Schule. (He got good grades in school.)
details.usage: Has two distinct meanings: censorship (suppression of information) and school grades (in some German regions); context determines the meaning.

details.etymology: Borrowed from French 'civilisation,' from Latin 'civilis' (relating to citizens), from 'civis' (citizen).
examples: Die moderne Zivilisation steht vor großen Herausforderungen. (Modern civilization faces great challenges.)
Viele alte Zivilisationen sind untergegangen. (Many ancient civilizations perished.)
details.usage: In German, a distinction is sometimes made between 'Zivilisation' (material/technical progress) and 'Kultur' (spiritual/artistic achievement), though this distinction has faded.

details.etymology: From Middle High German 'zuoversiht,' composed of 'zu' (to) and 'Versicht' (looking), related to 'versehen' (to foresee), meaning confident expectation.
examples: Trotz der Schwierigkeiten blickt sie mit Zuversicht in die Zukunft. (Despite the difficulties, she looks to the future with confidence.)
Seine Zuversicht hat ihn nie verlassen. (His confidence never left him.)
details.usage: A positive word expressing optimistic confidence; 'zuversichtlich' (confident, optimistic) is the derived adjective; the plural is extremely rare.

details.etymology: From 'beschließen' (to decide, resolve), composed of 'be-' and 'schließen' (to close), literally 'to close upon' a decision.
examples: Der Stadtrat hat einen wichtigen Beschluss gefasst. (The city council passed an important resolution.)
Der Beschluss wurde einstimmig angenommen. (The resolution was adopted unanimously.)
details.usage: A formal term for decisions and resolutions, especially in governmental and organizational contexts; 'einen Beschluss fassen' is the standard collocation.

details.etymology: From 'Sinn' (sense, mind) with the prefix 'ge-' and suffix '-ung,' referring to one's fundamental mindset or convictions.
examples: Er wurde wegen seiner politischen Gesinnung verfolgt. (He was persecuted for his political convictions.)
Ihre demokratische Gesinnung ist unbestritten. (Her democratic conviction is undisputed.)
details.usage: Often used in political and ethical contexts; 'Gesinnungsethik' (ethics of conviction, as opposed to 'Verantwortungsethik') is a concept from Max Weber.

details.etymology: From 'gut' (good) and 'achten' (to consider, regard), literally 'a good/careful consideration,' meaning an expert opinion or assessment.
examples: Das Gutachten des Experten war eindeutig. (The expert's report was conclusive.)
Ein medizinisches Gutachten wurde angefordert. (A medical assessment was requested.)
details.usage: Used in legal, medical, and technical contexts for expert reports; 'Gutachter' (expert, appraiser) is the person who writes the report.

details.etymology: Compound of 'Sache' (matter, thing) and 'Verhalt' (from 'verhalten,' behavior/state), meaning the state of affairs or facts of a case.
examples: Der Sachverhalt muss noch geklärt werden. (The facts of the matter still need to be clarified.)
Bitte schildern Sie den Sachverhalt. (Please describe the circumstances.)
details.usage: A formal and legal term for the factual situation or circumstances of a case; standard in police reports, legal proceedings, and official investigations.

details.etymology: From 'Stellung nehmen' (to take a position), composed of 'Stellung' (position) and 'Nahme' (taking), meaning a formal statement of opinion.
examples: Die Regierung gab eine offizielle Stellungnahme ab. (The government issued an official statement.)
Wir bitten Sie um eine Stellungnahme zu dem Vorfall. (We ask you for a statement regarding the incident.)
details.usage: A formal term for an official statement or position paper; commonly used in politics, law, and organizational communication.

details.etymology: From 'bewähren' (to prove oneself), related to 'wahr' (true), with 'be-' and '-ung,' meaning the proving or testing of oneself.
examples: Er wurde zu zwei Jahren auf Bewährung verurteilt. (He was sentenced to two years on probation.)
Die Bewährung der neuen Methode steht noch aus. (The new method still needs to prove itself.)
details.usage: Most commonly known in the legal phrase 'auf Bewährung' (on probation); also means proving oneself or a trial period in general usage.

details.etymology: From 'verdienen' (to earn, deserve), composed of 'ver-' and 'dienen' (to serve); as 'der Verdienst' it means earnings, as 'das Verdienst' it means merit.
examples: Sein Verdienst reicht kaum für die Miete. (His earnings are barely enough for the rent.)
Es ist ihr Verdienst, dass das Projekt erfolgreich war. (It is to her credit that the project was successful.)
details.usage: Note the gender distinction: 'der Verdienst' (masculine) means earnings/income, while 'das Verdienst' (neuter) means merit or credit — a rare case where gender changes meaning.

details.etymology: From 'heucheln' (to feign, be hypocritical), from Middle High German 'hūchelen,' possibly related to 'hocken' (to crouch), implying sneakiness.
examples: Seine Freundlichkeit war reine Heuchelei. (His friendliness was pure hypocrisy.)
Die Heuchelei der Politiker ist unerträglich. (The hypocrisy of politicians is unbearable.)
details.usage: A strong word with clearly negative connotations; 'Heuchler' (hypocrite) and 'heuchlerisch' (hypocritical) are common derived forms.

details.etymology: From 'verhalten' (to behave, relate), composed of 'ver-' and 'halten' (to hold), plus '-nis'; refers to how things relate to each other.
examples: Das Verhältnis zwischen den beiden Ländern hat sich verbessert. (The relationship between the two countries has improved.)
Er lebt in bescheidenen Verhältnissen. (He lives in modest circumstances.)
details.usage: Highly polysemous: means relationship, ratio, proportion, affair (romantic), or conditions/circumstances (in plural); context is essential.

details.etymology: Compound of 'wohl' (well) and 'Befinden' (condition, state of health), from 'sich befinden' (to feel, be situated).
examples: Regelmäßige Bewegung steigert das Wohlbefinden. (Regular exercise increases well-being.)
Ihr Wohlbefinden liegt uns am Herzen. (Your well-being is important to us.)
details.usage: An uncountable noun with no real plural; increasingly used in wellness and health promotion contexts alongside the English loanword 'Wellness.'

details.etymology: Compound of 'Zuflucht' (refuge, from 'zu' + 'Flucht' meaning 'flight toward') and 'Ort' (place), literally a 'place of refuge.'
examples: Die Bibliothek war sein Zufluchtsort. (The library was his place of refuge.)
Viele Flüchtlinge suchen einen sicheren Zufluchtsort. (Many refugees seek a safe haven.)
details.usage: Used both literally for physical places of shelter and figuratively for emotional or spiritual refuges; 'Zuflucht' alone is also common.
